# Extensible Longevity Model (ELM) -- Complete Slide Content
# https://silmonbiggs.github.io/extensible-longevity-model/
#
# This file contains the full text content of every slide in the
# interactive presentation, extracted for LLM accessibility.
# The interactive version includes Cytoscape network diagrams,
# clickable nodes, and dynamically rendered figures.
#
# Total slides: 61
# Generated from: docs/deck.html

## Scope of Evaluation

The active model -- the part that makes predictions and should be
evaluated -- consists of:

  - 8 coupled ODE subsystems (heteroplasmy, NAD+, DNA damage,
    AMPK/mTORC1, autophagy, senescence, SASP, methylation)
  - 6 ITP compound calibrations (12 sex-specific targets)
  - 4 sex-difference mechanisms (AMPK saturation, PK, testosterone, estrogen)
  - Dose-response Hill curves (3 fitted, 3 estimated)
  - BioAge composite -> death threshold
  - Combination prediction engine (additive pathway merging + saturation)

The codebase also contains two modules that are INACTIVE in all current
predictions and should not factor into evaluation:

  - Cancer module (elm/model.py: stochastic mutation accumulation).
    Cancer_prob is computed but NOT fed to BioAge; death is determined
    solely by BioAge >= death_threshold. The module is scaffolding for
    future calibration when cancer-specific endpoint data become available.

  - Cross-species translation (elm/uncertainty.py: human scaling).
    All current predictions are in mouse units (normalized lifespan).
    No mouse-to-human translation is applied or claimed.

Parameter census (Slide A16): ~150 total parameters break down as
26 literature-sourced, 78 estimated within physiological constraints,
39 structural/normalization constants, and ~25 inactive (cancer + human
scaling). The effective estimation problem is 10 DOF from 12 targets
(Slide A1), well-conditioned at kappa=14.6.

## Why a Quantitative Model

The biology of aging has accumulated decades of qualitative pathway
diagrams: arrows from protein to protein, marked "activates" or
"inhibits," with no magnitudes attached. You cannot predict whether
rapamycin + acarbose will be additive, sub-additive, or superadditive
by staring at arrows. You need rate constants, saturation curves, and
a system of equations that can be solved.

This model is offered as a productive starting point, not a ground
truth. It is designed to anneal toward agreement with biology through
an explicit experiment -> calibrate -> extend cycle (Slide A25).
The Taguchi killifish screen is the next turn of that cycle.

## SLIDE Intro 0: Six drugs extend mouse lifespan

Six drugs extend mouse lifespan. Which should we combine? About six drugs, given
individually to mice, make them live 5-26% longer. These results come from the
NIA Interventions Testing Program (ITP) -- a three-site, blinded lifespan study
using genetically heterogeneous (UM-HET3) mice, independently replicated at each
site. Compound What it does Male Female Rapamycin Slows cell growth signaling
+23% +26% Acarbose Blocks sugar absorption +22% +5% 17alpha-Estradiol Activates
energy-sensing pathway +19% 0% Canagliflozin Blocks sugar reabsorption in kidney
+14% +9% Aspirin Reduces inflammation +8% 0% Glycine Amino acid antioxidant +6%
+4% Notice: four of six drugs barely work -- or don't work at all -- in females.
Each was tested individually. Nobody knows what happens when you combine them.
Use -> arrow key or Next button to continue

--- Narrative ---
The ITP The Interventions Testing Program (ITP) is the gold standard for rodent
longevity research. Three independent labs test each compound simultaneously in
genetically diverse mice, so results aren't artifacts of one lab or one mouse
strain. These six compounds work through different biological pathways -- which
matters, because it means they're not all doing the same thing. The question is
whether combining drugs that work through different pathways could produce
larger effects than any single drug achieves alone.

## SLIDE Intro 1: Why single drugs hit a ceiling

[Figure: stage_A26_serial_reliability.png]

--- Narrative ---
Competing failure modes If aging had one dominant cause, a single drug targeting
that cause would show a huge effect. The fact that the best drugs only manage
+10-26% is the data speaking: aging has multiple independent failure modes, and
fixing one leaves the others untouched. The figure shows this with three failure
modes at different timescales. The organism dies from whichever strikes first.
Fix a later mode (B) while A remains -> almost no gain. The thin crescent
between the two curves is the entire signal. This is what single-drug testing
measures. Fix the earliest mode (A) -> moderate gain, but now B becomes the new
bottleneck. Fix A + B together -> mode C is finally revealed. It was always
there -- invisible behind the earlier walls. Three consequences Masking: Later
failure modes are invisible until you remove the earlier ones. Diminishing
returns: Fixing 1 of N modes gives at most ~1/N of the total possible extension.
You need combinations: Only by addressing multiple failure modes simultaneously
can you see what's behind the first wall. Figure uses Gaussian failure-time
distributions for clarity. Real failure modes follow different distributions,
but the masking principle is identical.

## SLIDE Intro 2: The same logic inside cells

The same competing-failures logic applies inside cells The previous slide used
organ-level examples (cancer, cardiovascular disease) to build intuition. The
same principle operates at the molecular level -- and that's where interventions
actually act. Aging involves at least four molecular deterioration processes,
each on its own timescale: Process What goes wrong Mitochondrial damage Mutant
mitochondrial DNA accumulates, impairing energy production DNA damage Oxidative
hits outpace repair; the repair machinery itself runs on NAD+, which is
declining Epigenetic drift The chemical marks that tell cells what to be
gradually scramble Senescent cell burden Damaged cells stop dividing but don't
die; they secrete inflammatory signals that damage neighbors Each of these can
become the binding constraint once the others are addressed -- just like the
organ-level failure modes on the previous slide. A drug that targets one process
leaves the others untouched. A model puts numbers to these systems. It maps all
four processes and the feedback loops between them, calibrated to the ITP drug
data, and predicts which combinations address the most failure modes -- and
which leave critical bottlenecks exposed.

--- Narrative ---
Why these four? These aren't arbitrary categories. Each corresponds to a
measurable biological variable that changes with age and responds to at least
one ITP compound. A model tracks how they interact -- for example, mitochondrial
damage depletes NAD+, which weakens DNA repair, which accelerates epigenetic
drift. A drug that boosts one subsystem can indirectly help the others. But this
also means the failure modes aren't fully independent. Rapamycin alone achieves
+23% precisely because it partially addresses multiple processes through a
central signaling hub. The model accounts for this coupling. Two questions
follow: (1) Can we test combinations efficiently? (2) Does the model actually
predict correctly? The next two slides address each.

## SLIDE Intro 3: How to test 8 interventions at once

There is a principled way to test 8 interventions in one experiment The ITP has
built an extraordinary single-compound foundation. But as the previous slides
showed, later failure modes are masked until the earlier ones are addressed. To
see what's underneath, you have to move what's on top -- and that requires
interventions acting on multiple failure modes simultaneously. A multi-
intervention experiment doesn't just measure interactions; it exposes partially
masked failure modes to measurement, and may reveal modes of aging not yet
detected. An orthogonal array arranges just 18 carefully chosen groups so that
each intervention's effect is cleanly separable from every other's. One
experiment yields: Main effect of each intervention Dose-response (two dose
levels per intervention) Dominant interactions -- which combinations show
synergy or redundancy The test organism: turquoise killifish -- a vertebrate
that lives only 4-6 months. The fastest vertebrate aging model available. $200K
total cost 8 mo timeline 1,000 killifish Main effects, dose-response curves, and
a screen for dominant interactions across 8 interventions -- from a single
experiment, at a fraction of the cost and time of testing one compound through
the ITP. The role of a mechanistic model: An orthogonal array tells you how to
test combinations efficiently. A model tells you which combinations matter most
-- focusing experimental resources on the highest-value questions. Full
orthogonal array and compound selection rationale in Slide A24.

--- Narrative ---
Model + Experiment Model and experiment are complementary: A model predicts
which pathways interact and where the bottlenecks are -- guiding experimental
priorities Experiments generate real combination data that validates or
invalidates a model's coupling assumptions Model predictions focus the screen;
screen results recalibrate the model Next: how well can a model do with existing
literature and data?

## SLIDE Intro 4: Does It Work?

We can't test every combination experimentally. So we built a model -- and it
correctly predicts a combination it has never seen. Calibrated to 6 individual
compounds. Then tested on a combination -- without refitting. Out-of-sample
validation: Rapamycin + Acarbose Model predicted +33.0% male lifespan extension.
ITP observed +34% . Error: 1.0 percentage point. Not fit -- predicted. 12
calibration targets +/-0.08 pp mean abs. error (predicted vs observed lifespan
extension) 10 effective parameters The model has fewer effective degrees of
freedom (10) than data points (12). It is constrained enough to make genuine
predictions, not just fit curves.

--- Narrative ---
Why This Matters A model that fits individual drugs but fails on combinations is
just curve-fitting. A model that predicts combinations is capturing real
biology. The rapamycin + acarbose prediction was never used during calibration.
Both compounds converge on the AMPK->autophagy pathway, producing Hill-function
saturation. The model correctly predicts sub-additive combination: 34%, not the
naive sum of 45%. Identifiability analysis (SVD of the Jacobian) confirms 10
observable degrees of freedom with condition number kappa = 14.6 -- well-
separated, not overfitting.

[Additional figures: stage_08_mixing_bars.png]

## SLIDE Intro 5: A Testable Prediction

The model makes a testable prediction: Rapamycin + NMN should be superadditive
These two compounds together do more than the sum of their parts. Scenario Male
Female Rapamycin alone +23.0% +26.2% NMN alone +3.4% +3.4% Simple sum +26.4%
+29.6% Model prediction +31.8% +36.0% Why superadditive? NMN's NAD+ boost (3.4%
alone) compounds over the extra lifespan that rapamycin provides. The longer the
animal lives, the more cumulative benefit from sustained NAD+ support. The
superadditive component (+5-6%) is structural, not a fitting artifact. Caveat:
The additive floor (+26% M / +30% F) is the defensible prediction. The full
prediction assumes compound effects persist into late life at mid-life
magnitude. Both compounds modeled from 9-month start. How does a model predict
this? The next slides build it, one subsystem at a time.

--- Narrative ---
Why This Combination? Rapamycin and NMN target independent subsystems :
Rapamycin: mTORC1 inhibition + AMPK activation -> autophagy, methylation
restraint NMN: NAD+ precursor -> supports SIRT1, PARP1 repair, mitochondrial
function Mechanistic independence means less pathway saturation when combined.
This is the opposite of rapamycin + acarbose, which both converge on AMPK and
produce sub-additive results. This prediction is robust to BioAge weight
variation: less than 0.5% shift across the plausible w meth range [0.25, 0.40].

## SLIDE 0: The Thesis

--- Narrative ---
The Thesis The model has four subsystems. We start with the one that turns out
to be the biggest bottleneck: mitochondrial heteroplasmy. Mitochondrial DNA
mutations accumulate with age as heteroplasmy (H) increases. H impairs oxidative
phosphorylation (OXPHOS), reducing NAD+ regeneration, and generates reactive
oxygen species (ROS) that cause oxidative DNA damage. The model posits that H
accounts for 25% of biological age (w H =0.25), but its real impact is larger --
it drives the downstream cascade through the other subsystems introduced in the
next six stages. Normalized age: t = 1.0 corresponds to median control lifespan
(~30 months for UM-HET3 males). All dynamics are expressed in this normalized
time. Degrees of freedom: 196 numbers define the model, but SVD analysis (Slide
A1) shows the ITP data only constrains 10 independent directions in parameter
space. The rest are set by published biology or don't affect predictions. This
is a 10-parameter model wearing a 196-parameter coat.

--- Equation ---
$$\text{BioAge} = w_H \frac{H}{N_H} + \text{(other terms introduced in Stages 1-6)}$$ $$\begin{aligned} w_H &= 0.25 &\quad \%\; &\text{heteroplasmy weight} \\ N_H &= 0.18 &\quad \%\; &\text{heteroplasmy norm (derived from control)} \end{aligned}$$The full equation with all four components is shown in Stage 7.

[Additional figures: stage_00_thesis.png]

## SLIDE 1: Heteroplasmy Dynamics

--- Narrative ---
Heteroplasmy Dynamics Mitochondrial DNA mutations accumulate through random
genetic drift (Kowald-Kirkwood model). Shorter mutant molecules replicate
faster, giving them a transcription advantage . These fast-replicating mutants
fail to encode proteins that are metabolically necessary, making their
mitochondria less efficient at energy conversion. Counteracting this:
FOXO3-dependent mitophagy (forkhead box O3 -- a transcription factor that
targets damaged mitochondria for recycling) selectively removes damaged
mitochondria. But as NAD+ falls -> SIRT1 drops -> FOXO3 weakens -> selection
against mutants fails -> H accelerates. This balance of accumulating and
eliminating damaged mitochondria plays out in the Heteroplasmy block (H). It is
a positive-feedback loop that progressively erodes the cell's ability to convert
fuel into energy. Rising heteroplasmy is progressive energy failure -- the prime
mover of aging in this model.

--- Equation ---
$$\frac{dH}{dt} = \left[\frac{H(1-H)}{N_{\text{eff}}} + \mu \cdot H(1-H) - s \cdot H\right] \times \tau$$$$s = s_0 + s_{\text{foxo3}} \cdot \text{FOXO3}$$ $$\begin{aligned} N_{\text{eff}} &= 100 &\quad \%\; &\text{effective mtDNA population} \\ \mu &= 0.20 &\quad \%\; &\text{transcription advantage} \\ s_0 &= 0.001 &\quad \%\; &\text{baseline selection} \\ s_{\text{foxo3}} &= 0.10 &\quad \%\; &\text{FOXO3 selection boost} \\ \tau &= 12.0 &\quad \%\; &\text{time scale} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_01_heteroplasmy.png]

## SLIDE 2a: NAD+ Supply

--- Narrative ---
NAD+ Supply NAD+ is the cell's energy currency for signaling and repair. Its
supply depends on NAMPT (nicotinamide phosphoribosyltransferase), the rate-
limiting synthesis enzyme, which declines with age. Heteroplasmy compounds the
problem: impaired OXPHOS reduces NAD+ regeneration from NADH, draining the pool
from both sides.

--- Equation ---
$$\frac{d[\text{NAD}]}{dt} = \underbrace{k_{\text{nampt}}\,e^{-k_{\text{age}}\,t}(1 + k_{\text{ampk}} \cdot \text{AMPK})}_{\text{supply}} - \;(\text{consumption terms in next stage})$$ $$\begin{aligned} k_{\text{nampt}} &= 0.55 &\quad \%\; &\text{NAMPT synthesis rate} \\ k_{\text{age}} &= 0.431 &\quad \%\; &\text{age-related decline (exponential)} \\ k_{\text{ampk}} &= 0.50 &\quad \%\; &\text{AMPK boost to synthesis} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_01_nad_decline.png]

## SLIDE 2b: NAD+ Consumption

--- Narrative ---
NAD+ Consumption As NAD+ supply drops, demand rises. CD38 , an ectoenzyme that
consumes NAD+, increases with age and inflammation. Meanwhile, SIRT1 -- a
NAD+-dependent deacetylase critical for gene silencing, stress response, and
mitophagy -- loses activity as its cofactor disappears. The result: a widening
gap between NAD+ supply and demand that accelerates with age.

--- Equation ---
$$\text{Consumption} = k_{\text{basal}} + \text{CD38} + k_{\text{parp}} \cdot \text{Damage} + k_{\text{sirt}} \cdot \text{SIRT1}$$$$\text{SIRT1} = \frac{\text{NAD}^{n_s}}{K_{s}^{n_s} + \text{NAD}^{n_s}}$$ $$\begin{aligned} k_{\text{basal}} &= 0.40 &\quad \%\; &\text{basal consumption} \\ k_{\text{parp}} &= 0.70 &\quad \%\; &\text{PARP1 consumption} \\ k_{\text{sirt}} &= 0.10 &\quad \%\; &\text{SIRT1 consumption} \end{aligned}$$

## SLIDE 3: PARP1 / Repair Fails

--- Narrative ---
PARP1 / Repair Fails Heteroplasmy generates ROS , which inflict oxidative DNA
damage. DNA repair itself depends on NAD+: base-excision repair scales as
(NAD/NAD_0) 0.7 , so as NAD+ falls, repair capacity drops and damage
accumulates. Accumulated damage consumes NAD+ via PARP1 (poly(ADP-ribose)
polymerase 1; k parp = 0.70, the largest NAD+ consumption coefficient). PARP1 is
both the primary NAD+ consumer and the enzyme that initiates repair -- the same
molecule creates the vicious cycle. The red inhibitory link from DNA_Damage to
the sum node below NAD closes this loop: NAD v -> PARP1/Repair v -> Damage ^ ->
PARP1 activation ^ -> NAD vv. The model assigns DNA damage a 25% weight in
BioAge.

--- Equation ---
$$\frac{d[\text{Damage}]}{dt} = k_{\text{ros}} \cdot \text{ROS} \cdot (1 - k_{\text{aox}} \cdot \text{aox}) - k_{\text{ber}} \left(\frac{\text{NAD}}{\text{NAD}_0}\right)^{n_{\text{ber}}} \!\cdot\, \text{Damage}$$ $$\begin{aligned} k_{\text{ros}} &= 0.60 &\quad \%\; &\text{ROS damage rate} \\ k_{\text{ber}} &= 0.35 &\quad \%\; &\text{base excision repair} \\ k_{\text{aox}} &= 0.55 &\quad \%\; &\text{antioxidant efficacy} \\ n_{\text{ber}} &= 0.7 &\quad \%\; &\text{NAD-repair exponent} \\ \text{ROS} &= 1 + 0.5\,H &\quad \%\; &\text{from heteroplasmy} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_02_dna_damage.png]

## SLIDE 4: The Signaling Hub

--- Narrative ---
The Signaling Hub AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK), the cell's energy sensor,
phosphorylates TSC2 (tuberous sclerosis complex 2), which inhibits Rheb and
shuts down mTORC1 (mechanistic target of rapamycin complex 1). mTORC1 inhibition
boosts autophagy (cellular self-recycling). Drug inhibition (rapamycin) and TSC2
inhibition are mechanistically independent and stack multiplicatively .

--- Equation ---
$$\text{TSC2} = b_0 + b_{\max} \cdot \frac{\text{AMPK}^n}{K_m^n + \text{AMPK}^n}$$$$\text{mTORC1} = \left(1 - \alpha \cdot \frac{\text{TSC2}^{n_2}}{K_{m2}^{n_2} + \text{TSC2}^{n_2}}\right)(1 - d_{\text{drug}})$$$$\text{Autophagy} = \frac{V_{\max} \cdot u}{K_a + u}, \quad u = w_1\!\cdot\!\text{AMPK} + w_2\!\cdot\!\text{FOXO3} + w_3\!\cdot\!\text{gut} + \Delta_{\text{UPR}} + \Delta_{\text{mTOR}}$$ $$\begin{aligned} b_0 &= 0.30 &\quad \%\; &\text{TSC2 basal} \\ b_{\max} &= 0.70 &\quad \%\; &\text{TSC2 max AMPK response} \\ K_m &= 0.35 &\quad \%\; &\text{TSC2 Hill half-max} \\ n &= 1.5 &\quad \%\; &\text{TSC2 Hill coefficient} \\ \alpha &= 0.65 &\quad \%\; &\text{max inhibition of mTORC1} \\ K_{m2} &= 0.50 &\quad \%\; &\text{mTORC1 Hill half-max} \\ n_2 &= 1.2 &\quad \%\; &\text{mTORC1 Hill coefficient} \\ K_a &= 0.35 &\quad \%\; &\text{autophagy half-max} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_03_autophagy.png]

## SLIDE 5: The Amplifier

--- Narrative ---
The Amplifier Damaged cells become senescent and secrete a senescence-associated
secretory phenotype (SASP). This creates a vicious cycle: SASP upregulates CD38,
adds oxidative damage, and drives paracrine senescence. The model assigns
senescent cells a 15% weight in BioAge. Caveat: No ITP compound is a senolytic,
so senescence/SASP parameters are literature-estimated, not calibrated to
lifespan data. The SASP feedback loop is structurally correct but quantitatively
unconstrained -- see Slide A4 for a full analysis of what this means for
predictions.

--- Equation ---
$$\frac{d[\text{Sen}]}{dt} = k_{\text{dmg}}(\text{Damage} - D_{\text{thresh}})^{+} + k_{\text{sasp}} \cdot \text{SASP} \;\ldots$$$$\ldots\; - \;(k_{\text{nat}} + k_{\text{auto}} \cdot \text{Autophagy}) \cdot \text{Sen}$$ $$\begin{aligned} k_{\text{dmg}} &= 0.40 &\quad \%\; &\text{damage-driven entry} \\ D_{\text{thresh}} &= 0.30 &\quad \%\; &\text{damage threshold} \\ k_{\text{sasp}} &= 0.20 &\quad \%\; &\text{SASP paracrine entry} \\ k_{\text{nat}} &= 0.02 &\quad \%\; &\text{natural clearance} \\ k_{\text{auto}} &= 0.70 &\quad \%\; &\text{autophagy clearance} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_04_senescence.png]

## SLIDE 6: The Clock

--- Narrative ---
The Clock DNA methylation drifts with age -- the basis of epigenetic clocks.
Three writers drive accumulation: EZH2 (histone methyltransferase): damage-
dependent -- more DNA damage -> more aberrant methylation DNMT (DNA
methyltransferase): age-dependent baseline drift SASP : inflammation accelerates
epigenetic drift One eraser: TET enzymes demethylate proportionally to current
methylation level. SIRT1 and AMPK restrain drift by inhibiting EZH2. As NAD+
falls -> SIRT1 drops -> EZH2 rises -> methylation accelerates. The model assigns
methylation a BioAge weight of 30% , tied with DNA damage as the joint-largest
component.

--- Equation ---
$$\frac{d\,\text{Meth}}{dt} = \underbrace{k_{\text{ezh2}} \!\cdot\! \text{EZH2} \!\cdot\! (0.1 + \text{Dmg})}_{\text{damage-dependent}} + \underbrace{k_{\text{dnmt}}}_{\text{baseline}} + \underbrace{k_{\text{sasp}} \!\cdot\! \text{SASP}}_{\text{inflammation}} - \underbrace{k_{\text{tet}} \!\cdot\! \text{Meth}}_{\text{erasure}}$$$$\text{EZH2} = \text{clamp}\!\left(1 - 0.5 \cdot \text{SIRT1} - k_{\text{ampk}} \cdot \text{AMPK},\; 0.1,\; 1.0\right)$$ $$\begin{aligned} k_{\text{ezh2}} &= 1.85 &\quad \%\; &\text{EZH2 methylation rate} \\ k_{\text{dnmt}} &= 0.28 &\quad \%\; &\text{DNMT baseline drift} \\ k_{\text{sasp}} &= 0.42 &\quad \%\; &\text{SASP-driven drift} \\ k_{\text{tet}} &= 0.02 &\quad \%\; &\text{TET demethylation} \\ k_{\text{ampk}} &= 0.70 &\quad \%\; &\text{AMPK inhibition of EZH2} \\ \text{Meth}_0 &= 0.25 &\quad \%\; &\text{youthful methylation} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_05_intermediates.png]

## SLIDE 7: The Aging System

--- Narrative ---
The Aging System The six subsystems from Stages 1-6 feed into a single composite
measure of biological age: BioAge = 0.30*Meth + 0.30*Damage + 0.25*H +
0.15*SenCells Each component is normalized so that BioAge = 1.0 at the control
animal's natural death. An animal dies when BioAge reaches this threshold. The
weights are model assumptions , constrained by calibration data. Methylation and
damage are weighted equally at 0.30, reflecting their coupled importance through
the AMPK and PARP/NAD pathways. Senescence carries the lowest weight because no
senolytic ITP data yet constrains it. The next six stages introduce compounds
that intervene on these subsystems. The first two -- rapamycin and acarbose --
target the mTORC1/AMPK signaling hub. The next three reduce oxidative damage
through different upstream paths. The last targets inflammation directly. Then
we calibrate.

--- Equation ---
$$\text{BioAge} = w_m \frac{\text{Meth}}{N_m} + w_d \frac{\text{Damage}}{N_d} + w_H \frac{H}{N_H} + w_s \frac{\text{SenCells}}{N_s}$$ $$\begin{aligned} w_m &= 0.30 &\quad \%\; &\text{methylation weight} \\ w_d &= 0.30 &\quad \%\; &\text{damage weight} \\ w_H &= 0.25 &\quad \%\; &\text{heteroplasmy weight} \\ w_s &= 0.15 &\quad \%\; &\text{senescence weight} \\ N_m &= 1.09 &\quad \%\; &\text{methylation at t=1.0 in control} \\ N_d &= 0.67 &\quad \%\; &\text{damage at t=1.0 in control} \\ N_H &= 0.18 &\quad \%\; &\text{heteroplasmy at t=1.0 in control} \\ N_s &= 0.22 &\quad \%\; &\text{senescence at t=1.0 in control} \end{aligned}$$

## SLIDE 8.1: Rapamycin

--- Narrative ---
8.1 Rapamycin The 'grow now' signal silencer -- turning down mTORC1 tells cells
to clean house instead of building new stuff. The only compound that directly
inhibits mTORC1 (via FK506-binding protein 12, FKBP12). Also activates AMPK
indirectly. Two independent pathways stack multiplicatively : drug inhibition on
mTORC1 plus AMPK->TSC2 inhibition. NIA Interventions Testing Program (ITP) male:
+23% lifespan extension.

--- Equation ---
$$\text{mTORC1} = (1 - I_{\text{TSC2}})(1 - d_{\text{drug}})$$$$\text{AMPK}_{\text{treated}} = \text{AMPK}_{\text{ctrl}} + \text{ampk}_{\text{direct}}$$ $$\begin{aligned} d_{\text{drug}} &= 0.84 &\quad \%\; &\text{mTORC1 direct inhibition (FKBP12)} \\ \text{ampk}_{\text{direct}} &= 0.56 &\quad \%\; &\text{AMPK boost} \to I_{\text{TSC2}} \text{ (Stage 4)} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_06_rapa_states.png, stage_06_rapa_bar.png]

## SLIDE 8.2: Acarbose

--- Narrative ---
8.2 Acarbose The starvation mimic -- slowing sugar absorption makes cells think
food is scarce, triggering maintenance programs. alpha-glucosidase inhibitor.
Delays carbohydrate absorption, activating AMPK via energy stress. Also
modulates gut microbiome composition. Gut microbiome changes reduce CD38
(protecting NAD+), inhibit DNMT (DNA methyltransferase) activity, and reduce
senescent cell accumulation. ITP male: +22% lifespan extension.

--- Equation ---
$$\text{AMPK}_{\text{eff}} = f(\text{ampk}_{\text{direct}} + \text{other inputs})$$$$\text{Target}_i \times \max\!\left(c_i,\; 1 - \text{gut} \cdot k_{\text{gut},i}\right) \quad i \in \{\text{CD38},\, \text{DNMT},\, \text{SenCells}\}$$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{ampk}_{\text{direct}} &= 0.44 &\quad \%\; &\text{AMPK activation} \\ \text{gut} &= 0.32 &\quad \%\; &\text{gut microbiome effect} \\ k_{\text{gut,cd38}} &= 0.75 &\quad \%\; &\text{CD38 reduction (protects NAD+)} \\ k_{\text{gut,dnmt}} &= 0.45 &\quad \%\; &\text{DNMT inhibition (slows methylation)} \\ k_{\text{gut,sen}} &= 0.40 &\quad \%\; &\text{senescence entry reduction} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_06_acarb_states.png, stage_06_acarb_bar.png]

## SLIDE 8.3: Canagliflozin

--- Narrative ---
8.3 Canagliflozin The glucose dumper -- excreting sugar in urine reduces the
fuel that generates damaging oxygen radicals. SGLT2 (sodium-glucose
cotransporter 2) inhibitor. Creates caloric deficit via glucose excretion,
activating AMPK mildly. Primary effect is antioxidant (reduced mitochondrial
ROS). ITP male: +14% lifespan extension.

--- Equation ---
$$\text{ROS}_{\text{eff}} = \text{ROS} \cdot (1 - k_{\text{aox}} \cdot \text{aox})$$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{ampk}_{\text{direct}} &= 0.15 &\quad \%\; &\text{AMPK activation} \\ \text{aox} &= 0.88 &\quad \%\; &\text{antioxidant strength} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_06_cana_states.png, stage_06_cana_bar.png]

## SLIDE 8.4: 17\u03B1-Estradiol

--- Narrative ---
8.4 17alpha-Estradiol The male-only energy sensor -- a non-feminizing estrogen
that boosts AMPK, but requires testosterone to work. Non-feminizing estrogen.
Activates AMPK and provides antioxidant protection. Requires testosterone
substrate (5alpha-reductase pathway) -- male-only efficacy. PMID 27312235 --
14.4 ppm in diet. ITP male: +19%, female: 0%

--- Equation ---
$$\text{ROS}_{\text{eff}} = \text{ROS} \cdot (1 - k_{\text{aox}} \cdot \text{aox})$$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{ampk}_{\text{direct}} &= 0.42 &\quad \%\; &\text{AMPK activation} \\ \text{aox} &= 0.46 &\quad \%\; &\text{antioxidant strength} \end{aligned}$$Requires testosterone (male-only)

[Additional figures: stage_06_17ae2_states.png, stage_06_17ae2_bar.png]

## SLIDE 8.5: Glycine

--- Narrative ---
8.5 Glycine The simplest shield -- an amino acid that soaks up reactive oxygen
species before they damage DNA. Amino acid supplement. Primarily antioxidant
with very mild AMPK activation. Smallest effect among the six compounds. ITP
male: +6%, female: +4%

--- Equation ---
$$\text{ROS}_{\text{eff}} = \text{ROS} \cdot (1 - k_{\text{aox}} \cdot \text{aox})$$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{aox} &= 0.54 &\quad \%\; &\text{antioxidant strength} \\ \text{ampk}_{\text{direct}} &= 0.04 &\quad \%\; &\text{AMPK activation} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_06_glycine_states.png, stage_06_glycine_bar.png]

## SLIDE 8.6: Aspirin

--- Narrative ---
8.6 Aspirin The inflammation brake -- quieting the alarm that senescent cells
broadcast to their neighbors. Cyclooxygenase (COX) inhibitor with antioxidant
and anti-inflammatory effects. Modest AMPK activation. Male-only at this dose --
sex mechanism explained in Stage 13. ITP male: +8%, female: 0% All six compounds
now visible. Next: calibration results.

--- Equation ---
$$\text{ROS}_{\text{eff}} = \text{ROS} \cdot (1 - k_{\text{aox}} \cdot \text{aox})$$$$\text{SASP}_{\text{eff}} = \text{SASP} \cdot (1 - k_{\text{ai}})$$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{aox} &= 0.20 &\quad \%\; &\text{antioxidant strength} \\ \text{ampk}_{\text{direct}} &= 0.12 &\quad \%\; &\text{AMPK activation} \\ k_{\text{ai}} &= 0.09 &\quad \%\; &\text{anti-inflammatory} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_06_aspirin_states.png, stage_06_aspirin_bar.png]

## SLIDE 9: Calibration

--- Narrative ---
Male Calibration Model calibrated to male ITP data: Compound ITP Model Rapamycin
+23% +23.0% Acarbose +22% +22.0% Canagliflozin +14% +13.8% 17alphaE2 +19% +19.0%
Aspirin +8% +7.8% Glycine +6% +6.0% BioAge -> death: t death = time when
BioAge(t) = 1.0. Logistic sigmoid (sigma = 0.12) models cohort variability.
Overfit? 10 effective DOF for 12 data points (Slide A1). The real test: an out-
of-sample combination prediction.

--- Equation ---
$$S(t) = \frac{1}{1 + \exp\!\left(\frac{t - t_{\text{death}}}{\sigma}\right)}$$ $$\begin{aligned} \sigma &= 0.12 &\quad \%\; &\text{transition sharpness} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_07_male_calibration.png, stage_08_survival_curves.png]

## SLIDE 10: Prediction

--- Narrative ---
Rapamycin + Acarbose Independent validation. Model was NOT fit to combination
data. Both converge on AMPK->Autophagy. Hill saturation -> sub-additive
combination. Model: 33%. Naive sum: 45%. ITP observed: 34%. Both compounds
modeled from 9-month start, matching ITP combo protocol (Strong 2022). Not fit
-- predicted.

--- Equation ---
$$\text{Autophagy} = \frac{V_{\max} \cdot x}{K_m + x}$$$$f(a+b) $$\begin{aligned} K_m &= 0.35 &\quad \%\; &\text{autophagy half-max} \\ x &= \textstyle\sum_i w_i \cdot \text{input}_i &\quad \%\; &\text{weighted pathway sum} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_08_mixing_bars.png]

## SLIDE 11: Why Males?

--- Narrative ---
AMPK Sex Difference Female AMPK baseline is 1.4x male (estrogen -> LKB1, liver
kinase B1; PMID 21876548 ). Higher baseline -> closer to saturation ->
diminishing returns. The literature-derived parameter predicts ITP sex ratios
for acarbose, canagliflozin, glycine. Not fit to sex data -- predicted from
literature.

--- Equation ---
$$\text{scaling}_F = \frac{1}{1 + k \cdot (\text{baseline}_F - 1)}$$ $$\begin{aligned} k &= 11 &\quad \%\; &\text{saturation steepness (acarbose)} \\ \text{baseline}_F &= 1.4 &\quad \%\; &\text{female AMPK (rel. to male)} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_09_ampk_mf_bars.png]

## SLIDE 11b: The Saturation Curve

--- Narrative ---
The Saturation Curve Female mice start with 1.4x higher AMPK activity (estrogen
-> LKB1). On the saturation curve, that means females are already past the steep
part. Adding more AMPK activation produces diminishing returns -- the same drug
dose moves females less than males.

[Additional figures: stage_09_ampk_saturation.png]

## SLIDE 12: The Exception

--- Narrative ---
Rapamycin Pharmacokinetics (PK) Rapamycin breaks the AMPK pattern: F (+26%) > M
(+23%). Higher female AMPK baseline alone cannot explain greater female survival
-- it actually favors males. Something else must more than offset this. The
answer is absorption: females have 3.48x higher blood levels (cytochrome P450
3A, CYP3A; PMID 24341993 ). Greater mTORC1 inhibition overcomes the AMPK
saturation disadvantage. Demonstrates model extensibility .

--- Equation ---
$$\text{effective}_F = \text{dose} \times r_{\text{pk}}$$$$\text{mTORC1}_F = f(\text{effective}_F)$$ $$\begin{aligned} r_{\text{pk}} &= 3.48 &\quad \%\; &\text{female/male blood level ratio} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_10_rapa_dose_response.png, stage_10_mf_updated.png]

## SLIDE 13.1: 17\u03B1-Estradiol

--- Narrative ---
17alpha-Estradiol +19% male, 0% female. Why? 17alphaE2 activates AMPK and
provides antioxidant protection. But it requires testosterone as a substrate --
it works through 5alpha-reductase, which converts testosterone to
dihydrotestosterone (DHT). Without testosterone (females, castrated males),
there is zero effect . Castrated males lose all benefit ( Garratt 2018, PMID
29806096 ).

--- Equation ---
$$\text{effect}_F = 0 \quad (\text{no testosterone substrate})$$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{ampk}_{\text{direct}} &= 0.42 &\quad \%\; &\text{male only} \\ \text{aox} &= 0.46 &\quad \%\; &\text{male only} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_11a_testosterone.png, stage_11a_mf_cumulative.png]

## SLIDE 13.2: Aspirin

--- Narrative ---
Aspirin +8% male, 0% female. Why? Aspirin is redundant with or interferes with
estrogen's natural cardioprotective pathway . Estrogen activates
COX-2->prostacyclin (PGI_2) for atheroprotection in females; aspirin's COX
inhibition suppresses this. Evidence: deleting the PGI_2 receptor removes
estrogen's protective effect. In postmenopausal women on hormone replacement
therapy (HRT), concurrent non-steroidal anti-inflammatory (NSAID) use reversed
cardioprotection (OR 0.66->1.50). ( Egan 2004, Science ; Grosser 2006, PLOS Med
)

--- Equation ---
$$\text{effect}_F = 0 \quad (\text{COX inhibition blocks estrogen} \to \text{COX-2} \to \text{PGI}_2 \text{ pathway})$$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{aox} &= 0.20 &\quad \%\; &\text{male only} \\ \text{ampk}_{\text{direct}} &= 0.12 &\quad \%\; &\text{male only} \\ k_{\text{ai}} &= 0.09 &\quad \%\; &\text{male only} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_11b_estrogen_intf.png, stage_11b_mf_cumulative.png]

## SLIDE 13.3: Four Sex Mechanisms

--- Narrative ---
Four Sex Mechanisms Every compound's sex difference is now explained by one of
four mechanisms: * AMPK saturation limited (acarbose, cana, glycine) * PK
difference dominant (rapamycin) * Testosterone-dependent (17alphaE2) * Estrogen
interference (aspirin) Three are predicted from literature parameters , not fit
to sex data. One (PK) uses measured blood levels.

[Additional figures: stage_11_mf_complete.png]

## SLIDE 14: The Full Picture

--- Narrative ---
The Full Picture BioAge = 0.30*Meth + 0.30*Damage + 0.25*H + 0.15*SenCells The
model tracks 17 variables across 7 ODEs and 9 algebraic intermediates, governed
by 196 parameters. Of these, 28 are calibrated to ITP lifespan data (13 compound
intervention values, 4 BioAge weights, 5 sex-mechanism parameters, and 6 dose-
response Hill fits). The remainder break down as follows: Source Count Examples
* Literature 26 PARP1/SIRT1 NAD+ ratio, mtDNA N eff , rapa PK * Estimated 78
Coupling strengths + 6 dose-response estimates * Structural 39 Hill
coefficients, bounds, normalization, sigmoid * Inactive 25 Cancer module, human
translation scaling Six compounds tested individually, 4 sex mechanisms, and 1
out-of-sample combination prediction (rapa + acarbose: 33% predicted, 34%
observed). Click any node or compound for parameter details and references.

--- Equation ---
$$\text{BioAge} = w_m \frac{\text{Meth}}{N_m} + w_d \frac{\text{Damage}}{N_d} + w_H \frac{H}{N_H} + w_s \frac{\text{SenCells}}{N_s}$$ $$\begin{aligned} w_m &= 0.30 &\quad \%\; &\text{methylation weight} \\ w_d &= 0.30 &\quad \%\; &\text{damage weight} \\ w_H &= 0.25 &\quad \%\; &\text{heteroplasmy weight} \\ w_s &= 0.15 &\quad \%\; &\text{senescence weight} \\ N_m &= 1.09 &\quad \%\; &\text{methylation norm} \\ N_d &= 0.67 &\quad \%\; &\text{damage norm} \\ N_H &= 0.18 &\quad \%\; &\text{heteroplasmy norm} \\ N_s &= 0.22 &\quad \%\; &\text{senescence norm} \end{aligned}$$

## SLIDE 15: Combination Predictions

[Figure: stage_14_dose_matrix.png]

--- Narrative ---
Combination Predictions The heatmap shows all pairwise combinations of 6 ITP
compounds at 4 dose levels (0, 0.5x, 1x, 2x) -- a 24x24 dose-response matrix
with per-compound Hill dose-response curves . Lower-left triangle = Male ,
upper-right = Female . Each compound has a normalized Hill function f(d) = d n
(K n +1)/(K n +d n ) fitted to ITP multi-dose data where available. At 2x dose,
effective pathway activation ranges from f(2)=1.02 (acarbose -- fully saturated)
to f(2)=1.50 (glycine -- still climbing). The greedy subtraction charts use
progressive removal : start with all 6 compounds, remove the one contributing
least, repeat. With dose-response curves, doubling dose adds almost nothing :
All 6 at 2x (49.9% M) barely exceeds all 6 at 1x (49.8% M). Glycine is the only
compound with meaningful 2x headroom (f(2)=1.50). Rapa and acarbose are
essentially saturated at 1x.

[Additional figures: stage_14_greedy_male.png, stage_14_greedy_female.png, stage_14_greedy_male_2x.png, stage_14_greedy_female_2x.png]

## SLIDE 16: Risk-Benefit Analysis

[Figure: stage_15_optimal_cocktails.png]

--- Narrative ---
Risk-Benefit Optimization There's no free lunch -- higher doses and more
compounds carry side-effect risk . The question: what's the best combination at
each risk budget? The risk profile (right) assigns literature-grounded scores
(0-10) per compound per dose: Rapamycin 2x (risk 6.5): immunosuppression,
metabolic effects Acarbose 2x (risk 4.0): GI distress -- but dose-response curve
shows zero additional benefit Glycine 2x (risk 1.0): essentially harmless -- and
the only compound with meaningful 2x headroom (f(2)=1.50) The optimal
combinations (left) enumerate all 729 combinations (6 compounds x {0, 1x, 2x})
with per-compound Hill dose-response curves. Key finding: Dose-response curves
dramatically compress the 2x predictions. Acarbose and rapamycin are saturated
at 1x (f(2)~1.02 and 1.09 respectively). Doubling all 6 compounds adds <1
percentage point over 1x dosing. The optimal male strategy at low risk (<=5) is
Acarb + Aspirin + 2xGlycine (34%); at risk <=12, adding rapa + 2x17alphaE2
reaches 50%. Females: Rapamycin dominates due to the 3.48x PK advantage. Best
low-risk (Rapa + 2xGlycine, 32%) scales to 39% at high risk budgets. Risk scores
are semi-quantitative. Dose-response curves fitted to ITP multi-dose data (rapa,
acarbose, 17alphaE2) or estimated from pharmacology (cana, aspirin, glycine).

[Additional figures: stage_15_risk_profile.png]

## SLIDE 17: Adding Targets

[Figure: stage_16_whatif.png]

--- Narrative ---
The Heteroplasmy Bottleneck The ITP unisex cocktail (Rapa+Acarb+Cana+Gly at 1x
dose) extends male lifespan by 53%, but the BioAge decomposition (left) reveals
what limits further gains: at the treated animal's death, heteroplasmy dominates
BioAge -- nearly untouched by any ITP compound. The stacked bars show each
component's fractional contribution to BioAge at death: Control -- balanced
contributions matching the assigned weights (30/30/25/15). ITP base
(Rapa+Acarb+Cana+Glycine at 1x dose) -- the best 4-element unisex combination
(+53% M, +45% F, avg 49%). Methylation, damage, and senescence are substantially
reduced, but heteroplasmy is barely affected , rising to dominate BioAge at
death. + Novel targets -- adding a mitophagy enhancer (targeting H directly)
plus NAD + interventions and senolytics breaks through the heteroplasmy
bottleneck. This follows from series reliability : when a system fails upon any
component reaching its threshold, treating all components except one leaves that
untreated component as the binding constraint. The four ITP compounds act
primarily through overlapping AMPK/mTORC1 pathways, reducing methylation drift,
DNA damage accumulation, and senescent cell burden -- but none significantly
slows mitochondrial heteroplasmy. Adding Targets The bar chart (right) shows
predicted extensions when interventions targeting currently unaddressed pathways
are layered on the ITP-4 unisex base (+53% M): Mitophagy enhancer (Urolithin A):
+36% marginal over base -- the single largest predicted gain, consistent with H
as the primary bottleneck NMN (NAD + precursor): +20% marginal -- directly
supplements the NAD + pool depleted by rising H CD38 inhibitor : +12% marginal
-- reduces age-dependent NAD + consumption driven by SASP Senolytic (D+Q): +10%
marginal -- pulsed clearance of senescent cells (biweekly, Baker 2016) Novel
compound parameters are literature-estimated, not calibrated to lifespan data.
These predictions indicate which mechanisms contribute most to further
extension, not precise magnitudes. Circularity note: No ITP compound directly
targets heteroplasmy, so the H dynamics and its BioAge weight (w H =0.25) are
constrained by model structure and literature, not by the lifespan data. The
"biggest bottleneck" conclusion follows substantially from this weight
assignment. The BioAge weights (30/30/25/15) are moderately constrained priors
-- w meth is allowable over [0.25, 0.40] -- not tight posteriors. The
qualitative conclusion (heteroplasmy is underaddressed) is robust across this
range, but the quantitative dominance depends on the specific weights chosen.

[Additional figures: stage_16_bioage_composition.png]

## SLIDE 18: Limitations

--- Narrative ---
What This Model Cannot Do Every model is a simplification. Before interpreting
predictions, understand the boundaries: Hard limitations No toxicity prediction.
The model tracks aging biology, not pharmacology. It cannot predict side
effects, organ toxicity, maximum tolerated dose, or drug-drug interactions
mediated by CYP metabolism, protein binding, or off-target effects. A compound
that extends model lifespan by 50% might kill the animal through hepatotoxicity.
No human translation. All parameters are calibrated to UM-HET3 mice (30-month
lifespan, specific genetic background). Translating to humans requires
additional assumptions about pathway conservation, dose scaling, and species-
specific biology that this model does not make. No pharmacokinetics beyond dose-
response. The model applies interventions as continuous pathway perturbations
from a start time. It does not model absorption, distribution, metabolism,
elimination, tissue-specific concentrations, or pulsed dosing pharmacokinetics
(except for senolytics, which use a simplified pulse model). Structural
constraints 10 effective degrees of freedom (Slide A1). The ITP data constrains
10 directions in parameter space. The model cannot resolve finer distinctions --
e.g., it cannot distinguish between two compounds that activate the same 10
directions with similar magnitudes, even if their biology differs in ways the
model doesn't capture. SASP/senescence parameters are unconstrained (Slide A4,
Stage 5 caveat). No ITP compound is a senolytic. The senescence feedback loop is
structurally included but its parameters are literature-estimated, not
calibrated to lifespan outcomes. Predictions involving senolytics carry wider
uncertainty than ITP-validated compounds. Novel compound predictions are
directional, not quantitative. NMN, CD38 inhibitors, mitophagy enhancers, and
senolytics use literature-estimated pathway injections without dose-response
calibration. The model reliably predicts which mechanisms matter most (Stage 17)
but the absolute magnitudes are uncertain. What the model can do Predict
combination outcomes for ITP compounds (validated: rapa+acarbose within 1% of
observed). Rank intervention targets by predicted leverage (Slide A9-A10).
Identify which novel targets to test first and which pairs to prioritize (Slide
A10-A11). Explain sex differences through four mechanistic routes (Stage 13.3).

## SLIDE A1: Identifiability Analysis

[Figure: stage_A1_svd_spectrum.png]

--- Narrative ---
How Many Parameters Does the Data See? The ELM has ~150 parameters but only 12
calibration targets (6 compounds x 2 sexes). A Jacobian-SVD analysis reveals
that exactly 10 linear combinations of parameters are constrained by the ITP
data. The remaining ~140 directions have zero sensitivity -- the data cannot
distinguish changes along them. Two condition numbers, two questions: kappa =
14.6 (full space): The 10 observable DOF are well-separated. Best-constrained
direction is only 14.6x more observable than the worst. Are the 10 DOF well-
defined? Yes. kappa = 210 (observable subspace): Among the 18 individual
parameters with nonzero sensitivity, the strongest (heteroplasmy
transcription_advantage, sigma=251) is 210x more observable than the weakest
(Km_nad_sirt1, sigma=1.2). Can we pin down individual values? Moderately. The
model has fewer effective DOF (10) than data points (12), leaving room for
genuine prediction rather than curve-fitting. Two exact null directions exist:
k_nmn_synthesis and k_sirt_consumption form a gauge pair (their ratio is
constrained but individual values are not).

--- Equation ---
$$J = U \Sigma V^T \quad \text{where } J_{ij} = \frac{\partial (\text{extension}_i)}{\partial \theta_j}$$$$\text{rank}(J) = 10, \quad \kappa_{\text{full}} = \frac{\sigma_1}{\sigma_{10}} = 14.6, \quad \kappa_{\text{obs}} = 210$$ $$\sigma_1 = 251.4$$ &mdash; heteroplasmy dynamics $$\sigma_2 = 68.3$$ &mdash; mTOR signaling chain $$\sigma_3 = 31.0$$ &mdash; AMPK &rarr; methylation $$\sigma_{10} = 1.2$$ &mdash; sirtuin sensitivity

## SLIDE A2: Gauge Symmetry

[Figure: gauge_drift_ratios.png]

--- Narrative ---
Is the Model Just Fitting a Rate? A gauge symmetry would mean the background
aging rate can be freely rescaled while adjusting intervention effects to
compensate -- producing identical predictions. The ELM has a partial gauge
symmetry. Increasing transcription_advantage by 10% shifts all 12 predictions,
but the shift-to-baseline ratios vary by compound (0.151 to 0.207, a 37% spread
). If the model were merely fitting a single rate, these ratios would be
identical. The spread comes from nonlinear saturation : AMPK Michaelis-Menten
kinetics buffer acarbose against gauge drift, while linear pathways (glycine
antioxidant) track the drift closely. This confirms genuine mechanistic content
beyond simple rate rescaling.

--- Equation ---
$$\text{Drift ratio} = \frac{\Delta\text{ext}_i / \text{ext}_i}{\Delta\text{ext}_{\text{ctrl}} / \text{ext}_{\text{ctrl}}}$$ Canagliflozin M: 0.151 (most buffered) Acarbose M: 0.207 (least buffered) Spread: 37% (partial, not full)

## SLIDE A3: Leave-One-Out Vulnerability

[Figure: loo_jacobian_residuals.png]

--- Narrative ---
What If We Didn't Have This Compound? For each ITP compound, we project its
Jacobian sensitivity onto the row space of the remaining 5 compounds. The
fraction captured measures how well the other compounds constrain the held-out
prediction. All compounds are 79-99% captured. The model's pathway architecture
constrains predictions even for held-out compounds. Key findings: Glycine is
most vulnerable (79.2%): its uniquely antioxidant-heavy profile is not shared by
other compounds. Est. Leave-One-Out (LOO) error: 3-5%. 17alphaE2 is most implied
(99.9%): its male effect uses pathways shared with other compounds; its female
zero is categorical. Female predictions drive vulnerability for compounds with
sex mechanisms (rapa, acarbose, canagliflozin) -- sex parameters provide unique
sensitivity directions that vanish when the compound is removed. Estimated LOO
errors are bounded within ~5% for all compounds -- consistent with a 10-DOF
model fit to 12 targets.

--- Equation ---
$$\text{Captured}(i) = \frac{\|\text{proj}_{\text{row}(J_{-i})} J_i\|}{\|J_i\|}$$ Glycine: 79.2% &mdash; most vulnerable 17&alpha;E2: 99.9% &mdash; most implied Rapamycin: 95.4% &mdash; best-conditioned Leave-One-Out error bounded within ~5%

## SLIDE A4: SASP: The Phantom Node

--- Narrative ---
SASP: Fully Wired, Nearly Silent The SASP module computes 5 downstream effects
at every timestep: CD38 upregulation, paracrine senescence, ROS production,
immune suppression, and methylation drift acceleration. Four of these are
consumed by downstream state updates. The fifth (cd38_upregulation) is computed
but never consumed -- the SASP->CD38 pathway is mediated by the receiver-side
parameter k_cd38_sasp instead. But none of it matters quantitatively: perturbing
all sender couplings by 10x shifts predictions by at most 0.10% . SASP levels
are small in the ITP operating regime because these compounds attack upstream
damage, keeping senescent cell counts low. The SASP module is correctly
unconstrained. The ITP panel contains no senolytics. A senolytic compound would
change SASP's operating range and make these couplings observable. Until then,
SASP is a phantom node: structurally necessary for the feedback loop,
quantitatively silent. w_sasp = 0.0 in BioAge -- SASP has zero direct weight.
Its influence is entirely indirect, through CD38->NAD+, ROS->damage, and
paracrine->senescence. Forward infrastructure: These SASP and senescence modules
remain ready to calibrate once senolytic trial data (e.g., dasatinib+quercetin
ITP results) and CD38 inhibitor measurements become available. The structural
coupling is in place; only the parameter values are unconstrained.

## SLIDE A5: Rapa + NMN Prediction

--- Narrative ---
Rapa + NMN: A Testable Prediction Rapamycin (mTORC1 inhibition + AMPK) and NMN
(NAD+ precursor) target independent subsystems. The model predicts their
combination produces superadditive lifespan extension: Scenario Male Female
Rapamycin alone 23.0% 26.2% NMN alone 3.4% 3.4% Additive floor 26.4% 29.6% Model
prediction 31.8% 36.0% The +5-6% superadditive component arises from model
structure: NMN's NAD+ boost (3.4% alone) compounds over the extra lifespan that
rapamycin provides. The longer the animal lives, the more cumulative benefit NMN
delivers. Whether this persists in practice depends on late-life dynamics -- see
caveat below. Both compounds modeled from 9-month start (plausible ITP
protocol). [!] Late-life extrapolation caveat: The model assumes constant
intervention efficacy throughout life. In reality, autophagy capacity declines,
NAD+ production falls further, and senescent cell burden grows in late life. The
superadditive component relies on compound effects persisting at mid-life
magnitude into late life. The additive floor (26% M / 30% F) is the defensible
prediction. The full prediction (32% M / 36% F) is a structural upper bound
conditioned on late-life extrapolation.

--- Equation ---
$$\text{ext}_{\text{combo}} = \frac{t_{\text{death,combo}} - t_{\text{death,ctrl}}}{t_{\text{death,ctrl}}}$$$$\text{ext}_{\text{combo}} > \text{ext}_{\text{rapa}} + \text{ext}_{\text{NMN}}$$ Male: 31.8% (additive floor 26.4%) Female: 36.0% (additive floor 29.6%) Superadditivity: +5.4% (M), +6.4% (F) Both compounds from 9-month start (plausible ITP protocol)

## SLIDE A5b: Rapa + NMN Superadditivity Robustness

[Figure: stage_A5_sensitivity.png]

--- Narrative ---
Rapa + NMN Superadditivity Robustness Every parameter perturbed +/-20%. The
total Rapa+NMN prediction shifts by at most +/-1.5 pp. The superadditive
component (+5.5 pp baseline) survives all perturbations -- it never drops below
zero. Most sensitive parameter: k_nmn_synthesis (NMN->NAD efficiency, +/-1.5
pp). Second: Km_nad_sirt1 (NAD affinity for SIRT1, +/-1.3 pp). BioAge weights
shift the total prediction but barely affect the superadditive component.

## SLIDE A6: BioAge Weight Sensitivity

[Figure: w_meth_sweep.png]

--- Narrative ---
How Much Does Methylation Weigh? The BioAge composite weights each subsystem:
methylation (w meth =0.30), damage (0.30), heteroplasmy (0.25), senescence
(0.15). How sensitive are predictions to this weighting? A sweep across w meth
in [0.15, 0.55] reveals a sharp constraint: Below 0.20: Acarbose predictions
collapse (error >3%) Above 0.50: Rapa+acarbose validation degrades [0.25, 0.40]:
All calibration targets within 1%, validation holds Current: w meth = 0.30, mean
error 0.08% The rapa+NMN combination prediction is robust across this range:
<0.5% shift between w meth =0.25 and 0.40. The purple band: external constraint
from aging clocks How much should methylation weigh in a biological age
composite? We mapped the components of three published clocks -- GrimAge2 ( Lu
2022 ), PhenoAge ( Levine 2018 ), and DunedinPACE ( Belsky 2022 ) -- to ELM-
equivalent aging categories, and computed the epigenetic fraction of each
clock's mortality-predictive variance. The range is 5-30% : GrimAge's
adrenomedullin surrogate (enriched in Polycomb targets) contributes ~11% as
strict epigenetic drift, rising to ~15% when damage-coupled remodeling is
included. PhenoAge's RDW-dominated variance reaches ~30% under the broad
estimate. Pure drift clocks ( Horvath 2013 , Hannum 2013 ) capture ~40% of
composite-clock mortality signal ( Hillary 2020 ), but this is an upper bound
since drift clocks also partly read out non-epigenetic biology. The ELM's w meth
=0.30 sits at the top of this external range -- which is what the calibration
data demands. The model's methylation weight is pushed high because the ELM's
methylation ODE integrates damage-accelerated drift (via EZH2) and SASP-
accelerated drift, not just stochastic CpG noise. Analysis:
scripts/clock_pca/clock_methylation_fraction.py

--- Equation ---
$$\text{BioAge} = w_m \cdot \frac{\text{Meth}}{N_m} + w_d \cdot \frac{\text{Damage}}{N_d} + w_H \cdot \frac{H}{N_H} + w_s \cdot \frac{\text{SenCells}}{N_s}$$ w meth constrained to [0.25, 0.40] by calibration data Current: 0.30 (mean error 0.08%) Rapa+NMN prediction robust to weight variation (&lt;0.5%)

## SLIDE A6b: Weight Sensitivity: H & Sen

[Figure: stage_A6b_weight_sensitivity.png]

--- Narrative ---
How Robust Is the Bottleneck? The "heteroplasmy is the biggest bottleneck"
conclusion (Slide 17) depends on w H =0.25. But no ITP compound targets
heteroplasmy directly, so this weight is constrained by model structure and
literature, not by the lifespan data. How much does the weight assignment
matter? w hetero sweep (top row) Calibration constrains w H to [0.20, 0.30] --
outside this range, max ITP target error exceeds +/-1%. At w H =0.20 (lowest
acceptable): H still accounts for 46% of BioAge at death in treated animals, and
mitophagy marginal addition is +114% -- still the dominant novel target by a
wide margin. At w H =0.30 (highest acceptable): H accounts for 57%, mitophagy
marginal is +160%. The qualitative conclusion -- heteroplasmy is the primary
untreated bottleneck, and mitophagy is the most valuable novel target -- is
robust across the entire calibration-acceptable range . w sen sweep (bottom row)
Calibration constrains w sen to [0.10, 0.20] . Within this range, the H
bottleneck (51-53%) and mitophagy marginal (135-140%) barely move. Senescence
weight has minimal impact on the key conclusions. Remaining weights are
redistributed proportionally when a single weight is varied. The Rapa+Acarb
validation prediction tracks the calibration: it passes (+/-1pp of observed 34%)
only within the same weight ranges that pass individual calibration.

## SLIDE A7: Feedback Loops

--- Narrative ---
Feedback Loops The aging network contains three positive feedback (vicious
cycle) loops that amplify damage beyond what any single hallmark would predict.
The diagram highlights them in two colors. PARP->NAD Vicious Cycle (+3.0%) DNA
damage consumes NAD + via PARP1 activation, reducing repair capacity, causing
more damage. This self-amplifying cycle (red edges) is 15x more impactful than
either SASP loop. SASP Feedback Loops (+0.1-0.2%) Two SASP-mediated loops
(orange edges): CD38-mediated: SASP -> CD38 -> NADv -> PARP1/Repairv -> Damage^
-> Senescence^ Paracrine: SASP -> DNA damage -> Senescence -> more SASP SASP
loops are less impactful because SASP carries zero direct weight in BioAge (w
SASP =0); their damage is indirect, mediated through CD38 and paracrine
senescence. Key insight: Positive feedback means damage accelerates -- the
system gets worse faster the worse it already is. This explains why aging
accelerates in late life.

--- Equation ---
Three positive feedback loops:$$\begin{aligned}\color{#e74c3c}{\text{Loop 3:}} \quad & \text{Damage} \xrightarrow{k_{\text{parp}}} \text{NAD}\!\downarrow \;\to\; \text{PARP1/Repair}\!\downarrow \;\to\; \text{Damage} \\[4pt]\color{#e67e22}{\text{Loop 2:}} \quad & \text{SASP} \xrightarrow{k_{\text{cd38}}} \text{NAD}\!\downarrow \;\to\; \text{PARP1/Repair}\!\downarrow \;\to\; \text{Damage}\!\uparrow \\[4pt]\color{#e67e22}{\text{Loop 1:}} \quad & \text{Sen} \xrightarrow{k_{\text{sasp}}} \text{SASP} \xrightarrow{k_{\text{par}}} \text{Sen}\end{aligned}$$Loop-breaking lifespan gains (untreated control):$$\begin{aligned}\text{PARP-NAD} &: +3.0\% \\\text{SASP-NAD-Damage} &: +0.2\% \\\text{SASP Paracrine} &: +0.1\%\end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_17_loop_breaking.png]

## SLIDE A8: Hallmark Importance

--- Narrative ---
Hallmark Importance "What if this hallmark never deteriorated?" For each state
variable, we zero its driving forces (= perfect prevention) and measure the
lifespan extension. DNA Damage: +68% -- far exceeds its 25% BioAge weight. Why?
Because damage feeds back through the PARP1/Repair->NAD vicious cycle from Slide
A7 AND triggers senescence (another 15% of BioAge). Preventing damage has
cascading protective effects. Methylation: +36% -- proportional to its 30%
weight. Methylation is an autonomous clock with limited feedback coupling.
Heteroplasmy: +15% -- close to its 25% weight. H drives NAD + depletion via
OXPHOS failure, but the indirect effects are modest in isolation. Senescent
Cells: +14% -- close to its 15% weight, limited amplification. Key insight: DNA
Damage's +68% vs its 25% weight is the fingerprint of feedback amplification .
The PARP1/Repair->NAD vicious cycle (Slide A7) explains why: preventing damage
also preserves NAD + , which maintains PARP1/Repair capacity, which prevents
more damage -- a virtuous cycle.

--- Equation ---
$$S_i = \frac{\Delta\%\;\text{lifespan}}{\Delta\, p_i}\bigg|_{p_i=0.1}$$Node clamping results:$$\begin{aligned}\text{DNA Damage} &: +68\% \quad (w_d = 0.25) \\\text{Methylation} &: +36\% \quad (w_m = 0.30) \\\text{Heteroplasmy} &: +15\% \quad (w_H = 0.25) \\\text{SenCells} &: +14\% \quad (w_s = 0.15)\end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_17_node_clamping.png]

## SLIDE A9: Pathway Sensitivity

--- Narrative ---
Intervention Point Sensitivity For each intervention point, we apply a standard
dose (0.1) and measure the marginal lifespan gained per unit dose. The tornado
chart ranks the actionable targets: AMPK activation (62) is the top-ranked
intervention point -- consistent with AMPK's position as the master energy
sensor driving TSC2, autophagy, senescence reduction, and EZH2 inhibition
simultaneously. This is the hub through which most ITP compounds operate.
Senolytic (D+Q) (60) is nearly as effective. The model uses a pulsed clearance
mechanism: biweekly dosing (Baker et al. 2016, PMID 26840489 ), with ~30%
senescent cell clearance per pulse (Zhu et al. 2015, PMID 25754370 ). Repeated
cycles compound, cumulatively clearing the majority of senescent cells over the
treatment window. Gut microbiome (22) acts through CD38 inhibition, methylation
reduction, and senescence reduction -- three independent downstream effects.
Mitophagy (13) ranks highest among novel targets, reinforcing the Stage 17
finding that heteroplasmy is a key bottleneck. Strategic implication: The
greatest leverage is at DNA damage (node clamping +68%) and AMPK activation
(sensitivity 62). The PARP->NAD vicious cycle (Slide A7) suggests that NAD +
interventions (NMN, CD38 inhibitors) gain additional value by breaking this
feedback loop, beyond their direct NAD + replenishment. Senolytic benefit is
frequency-dependent; more aggressive dosing protocols would increase its
ranking.

--- Equation ---
$$S_i = \frac{\Delta\%\;\text{lifespan}}{\Delta\, p_i}\bigg|_{p_i=0.1}$$Top intervention point sensitivities:$$\begin{aligned}\text{AMPK activation} &: 62 \\\text{Senolytic (D+Q)} &: 60 \\\text{Gut microbiome} &: 22 \\\text{Mitophagy} &: 13 \\\text{mTORC1 inhibition} &: 7\end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_17_pathway_sensitivity.png]

## SLIDE A10: Pairwise Synergy

[Figure: stage_A10_synergy_full.png]

--- Narrative ---
Full Pairwise Synergy Matrix Single-point sensitivity (A9) ranks interventions
in isolation. But in a nonlinear system with feedback loops, the value of
intervention A depends on whether B is also active. This 10x10 matrix
systematically maps all 45 compound pairs as super-additive (red) or sub-
additive (blue). Reading the matrix: The diagonal shows each compound's solo
lifespan extension. Off-diagonal cells show synergy in percentage points -- how
much the pair exceeds (or falls short of) the sum of its parts. Key findings
Rapa + Acarbose: -6.0 pp (M) -- subadditive. Both compounds converge on AMPK,
which saturates via Michaelis-Menten kinetics. The marginal value of more AMPK
activation diminishes once TSC2 is near-maximally activated. Rapa + NMN: +7.0 pp
-- superadditive. mTORC1 inhibition (rapamycin) and NAD + replenishment (NMN)
attack independent rate-limiting steps. Rapamycin boosts autophagy while NMN
restores SIRT1-mediated DNA repair -- no shared bottleneck. Mitophagy +
everything: strongly superadditive -- +22 pp with Rapa (M), +17 pp with Acarb,
+15 pp with 17alphaE2. Heteroplasmy is a root-cause driver (Stage A8 node
clamping: +15%) that no ITP compound directly addresses. Adding mitophagy is
always pathway-independent from ITP mechanisms. 17alphaE2 is broadly subadditive
with ITP compounds (M) -- -5.5 with Rapa, -7.5 with Acarb. Its SIRT1-mediated
mechanism partially overlaps with NAD->SIRT1 benefits already provided by AMPK-
activating drugs. Sex differences: Female panel shows much weaker ITPxITP
interactions (most near zero) because sex modifiers already attenuate several
ITP compounds in females, leaving less signal to interact. Dashed lines separate
the 6 ITP-validated compounds (top-left block) from the 4 novel targets (bottom-
right). Cross-block cells show ITPxnovel synergies -- the most actionable
predictions for experimental design.

--- Equation ---
$$\Delta_{ij} = \text{ext}(i{+}j) - \text{ext}(i) - \text{ext}(j)$$Since ext({}) = 0, this is a proper interaction term.$$\Delta > 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{super-additive (pathway-independent)}$$$$\Delta < 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{sub-additive (shared bottleneck)}$$All 45 pairs x 2 sexes = 90 pairwise simulations + 10 singles x 2 sexes.

## SLIDE A11: Conditional Synergy

[Figure: stage_A10_synergy_conditional.png]

--- Narrative ---
Conditional Synergy: Novel Pairs on ITP Base The full matrix (A10) tests pairs
in isolation. But the real question for translational design is: given that the
ITP base cocktail (Rapa + Acarb + Cana + Glycine) is already active, which novel
target pairs add more than the sum of their individual additions? Diagonal =
marginal contribution of each novel target when added solo to the ITP base. Off-
diagonal = conditional synergy between novel target pairs, controlling for the
base. Key findings Mitophagy dominates -- +137.5% marginal addition (M), +101.5%
(F) on top of ITP base. This reflects the model's prediction that heteroplasmy
is the primary unaddressed bottleneck in the ITP cocktail. With AMPK/mTORC1
already saturated by the base, reducing mitochondrial mutant load unlocks a new
axis of benefit. Mitophagy + Senolytic: +29 pp conditional synergy (M) -- the
top novel pair. Mechanistically independent: mitophagy reduces heteroplasmy ->
less ROS -> less damage, while senolytics directly clear senescent cells.
Together they break both the upstream (mito damage) and downstream (senescence
accumulation) arms of the vicious cycle. NMN + CD38i: -10.5 pp (M) --
subadditive. Both target NAD + homeostasis (NMN boosts synthesis, CD38i reduces
consumption). On top of the ITP base which already activates AMPK->NAMPT,
further NAD + boosting saturates SIRT1 activation. Mitophagy + NMN: +33 pp (M),
+12.5 pp (F) -- strongly synergistic. Mitophagy reduces heteroplasmy, improving
OXPHOS efficiency and reducing NAD + consumption from ROS-induced damage. NMN
simultaneously boosts the now-more-efficient NAD + synthesis pathway. Each makes
the other more effective. Implication for experimental design: Prioritize
Mitophagy (urolithin A) + Senolytic (D+Q) as the first novel pair to test on top
of the ITP cocktail. The conditional synergy is large, mechanistically
interpretable, and both compounds are available for mouse studies. SASP
dependency warning: The senolytic predictions (diagonal and off-diagonal) depend
on SASP coupling parameters that are literature-estimated, not calibrated to
lifespan data (see A4). The Mitophagy+Senolytic synergy (+29 pp) is the most
SASP-dependent result in this matrix. The Mitophagy+NMN synergy (+33 pp) is more
robust as it operates through the NAD+/heteroplasmy axis.

--- Equation ---
$$\Delta_{ij|B} = \text{ext}(B{+}i{+}j) - \text{ext}(B{+}i) - \text{ext}(B{+}j) + \text{ext}(B)$$where $B$ = ITP base (Rapa + Acarb + Cana + Gly). This is the second-order interaction term conditioned on a nonzero baseline -- it isolates the novelxnovel interaction from the already-captured basexnovel effects.

## SLIDE A12: Network Structure (1/4): Adjacency

[Figure: stage_A10_adjacency.png]

--- Narrative ---
Weighted Adjacency Matrix The synergy matrices (A10-A11) show which pairs
interact. To explain why , we express the ODE coupling structure as a weighted
directed graph. Each cell A[i,j] is the coupling strength of node j's influence
on node i, extracted directly from the differential equations in model.py.
Reading the matrix Green cells = activating (e.g. NAD -> SIRT1: +0.9). More of
the column variable increases the row variable. Blue cells = inhibiting (e.g.
TSC2 -> mTORC1: -0.9). More of the column variable decreases the row variable.
Empty cells = no direct coupling. Influence can still propagate indirectly
through intermediaries. Key structures visible SASP vicious cycle: SenCells ->
SASP (+0.8), then SASP feeds back to NAD (-0.6), DNA damage (+0.4), SenCells
(+0.5), and Methylation (+0.4). This is the aging amplifier -- once triggered,
it accelerates multiple damage axes simultaneously. AMPK signaling cascade: AMPK
-> TSC2 (+0.8) -> mTORC1 (-0.9) fans out to Autophagy, DNA damage, and
Senescence. This chain explains why AMPK activation has the highest sensitivity
(A9). NAD->SIRT1->FOXO3 axis: A linear cascade (+0.9, +0.7) that branches into
autophagy, DNA repair, methylation, and heteroplasmy selection. NAD + depletion
propagates broadly. BioAge row: Only 4 direct inputs (Meth 0.30, DNA_Dmg 0.30,
Hetero 0.25, SenCells 0.15) -- the composite death clock.

--- Equation ---
$$A_{ij} = \text{ODE coupling strength of } j \text{ on } i$$Green: $A_{ij} > 0$ (activating) &emsp; Blue: $A_{ij} 15 state variables x 15 = 225 cells, of which 31 are nonzero -- the network is sparse (14% density), meaning most node pairs interact only indirectly.

## SLIDE A13: Network Structure (2/4): Node Degree

[Figure: stage_A10_degree.png]

--- Narrative ---
Node Degree: Influence Received vs Broadcast For each node, in-degree (red) sums
the absolute coupling weights it receives from other nodes. Out-degree (blue)
sums the weights it sends. This reveals which nodes are hubs (high out-degree)
vs integrators (high in-degree). Hub nodes (high out-degree) AMPK (out: ~2.3) --
Broadcasts to TSC2, NAD, Methylation, and SenCells simultaneously. This is why
AMPK activation ranks #1 in A9 sensitivity: perturbing it moves four downstream
variables at once. SASP (out: ~2.4) -- The inflammatory secretome fans out to
NAD (CD38), DNA damage (ROS), SenCells (paracrine), Methylation (drift), and
Immune (suppression). SASP is the primary aging amplifier. SIRT1 (out: ~1.9) --
Distributes NAD + status to FOXO3, DNA repair, methylation, and senescence. This
makes NAD + interventions (NMN, CD38i) broadly effective. Integrator nodes (high
in-degree) SenCells (in: ~2.4) -- Receives inputs from 7 nodes (mTORC1, SIRT1,
DNA damage, SASP, Autophagy, AMPK, DNA_Dmg). This convergence makes senescent
cell count a reliable biomarker -- it integrates the state of the whole system.
DNA_Dmg (in: ~1.6) -- Accumulates damage from ROS, SASP, and heteroplasmy,
tempered by SIRT1-mediated repair and mTORC1 proteostasis. Synergy link:
Compounds targeting high-out-degree nodes (AMPK, SIRT1) tend to be subadditive
with each other because their downstream effects overlap. Compounds targeting
pathway-independent nodes (e.g. Hetero + SenCells) are superadditive.

--- Equation ---
$$\text{In-degree}(i) = \sum_j |A_{ij}| \quad \text{(influence received)}$$$$\text{Out-degree}(j) = \sum_i |A_{ij}| \quad \text{(influence broadcast)}$$High out-degree = hub (perturbation propagates widely). High in-degree = integrator (state reflects many upstream signals).

## SLIDE A14: Network Structure (3/4): Bottlenecks

[Figure: stage_A10_betweenness.png]

--- Narrative ---
Betweenness Centrality: Bottleneck Nodes Betweenness centrality measures how
often a node sits on the shortest path between all other node pairs. High-
betweenness nodes are bottlenecks -- most information flow in the network passes
through them. Top bottleneck nodes SASP (1.00) -- Maximum betweenness. SASP sits
at the nexus of the senescence->inflammation->NAD depletion feedback loop.
Nearly every path from an intervention to a downstream damage variable passes
through SASP signaling. This explains why anti-inflammatory compounds have broad
but moderate effects. SenCells (0.95) -- The gateway between damage accumulation
and SASP production. Senolytics directly target this bottleneck, explaining
their high sensitivity score (60 in A9) and broad superadditivity in the synergy
matrix. SIRT1 (0.74) -- The main conduit for NAD + status to reach downstream
effectors (FOXO3, DNA repair, methylation). Disrupting SIRT1 availability (via
NAD + depletion) bottlenecks four repair pathways simultaneously. NAD (0.60) --
Upstream of SIRT1, NAD + is the metabolic bottleneck. Its high centrality
explains why NMN and CD38 inhibitors show synergy with pathway-independent
interventions (mitophagy, senolytics). Low-betweenness nodes BioAge (0.00) -- A
terminal node (no outgoing edges), so no paths pass through it. AMPK (0.00) --
Despite high out-degree, AMPK has zero betweenness because it's a source node:
paths originate from it but don't pass through it. Its leverage comes from fan-
out, not from being on others' paths. Structural explanation for synergy:
Pathway-independent synergy (green cells in A10) occurs when two interventions
target nodes whose shortest paths to BioAge do not share high-betweenness
intermediaries. Pathway overlap (blue cells) occurs when both must route through
the same bottleneck (e.g. SIRT1), creating diminishing returns via saturation.

--- Equation ---
$$C_B(v) = \sum_{s \neq v \neq t} \frac{\sigma_{st}(v)}{\sigma_{st}}$$$\sigma_{st}$ = number of shortest paths from $s$ to $t$ $\sigma_{st}(v)$ = number that pass through $v$ Computed via Floyd-Warshall on distance matrix $d_{ij} = 1/|A_{ij}|$, then normalized to [0, 1].

## SLIDE A15: Network Structure (4/4): Path Distances

[Figure: stage_A10_paths.png]

--- Narrative ---
Intervention -> BioAge: Path Distances For each drug class, we compute the
shortest weighted path from its entry point in the ODE network to the BioAge
composite output. Shorter paths mean more direct leverage; longer paths mean the
intervention must cascade through multiple intermediaries, introducing more
opportunities for nonlinear interactions. Results Methylation targets
(17alphaE2): dist = 2.9 -- The shortest path. Methylation feeds directly into
BioAge (w meth = 0.30), so EZH2/SIRT1-mediated methylation reduction translates
almost linearly to lifespan extension. This directness is why 17alphaE2 shows
less synergy with other compounds -- its effect doesn't depend on what else is
happening in the network. Heteroplasmy targets (Mitophagy): dist = 4.0 --
Moderate path length. Heteroplasmy contributes to BioAge both directly (w H =
0.25) and indirectly via ROS -> NAD depletion and DNA damage. The dual pathway
explains mitophagy's strong synergy: it reduces upstream damage that compounds
other interventions' benefits. AMPK (Rapa, Acarb, Cana): dist = 6.2 -- Longest
path. AMPK must cascade through TSC2 -> mTORC1 -> Autophagy/SenCells -> SASP ->
damage variables -> BioAge. Each hop introduces nonlinearity, which is why AMPK-
activating drugs show the strongest interactions (both positive and negative) in
the synergy matrix. NAD (NMN, CD38i): dist = 6.5 and SenCells (D+Q): dist = 6.6
-- Also indirect. NAD routes through SIRT1 -> FOXO3 -> multiple branches.
Senolytics route through SASP reduction -> downstream recovery. Both create
cascading benefits that interact strongly with other interventions. Design
principle: Combine a short-path drug (direct leverage, predictable effect) with
a long-path drug (cascade amplification, nonlinear boost). The A10 synergy data
confirms this: Mitophagy (dist=4) + Senolytic (dist=6.6) is the top pair, while
Rapa (dist=6.2) + Acarb (dist=6.2) overlap heavily and are subadditive.

--- Equation ---
Shortest path distance from intervention node $s$ to BioAge:$$d(s, \text{BioAge}) = \min_{\text{path}} \sum_{\text{edges}} \frac{1}{|A_{ij}|}$$Computed via Floyd-Warshall. Color coding: Red = direct (d 5)

## SLIDE A16: Model Specification

Model Specification A16 -- Complete mathematical specification of the ELM. All
equations and parameters needed to reproduce the model.

--- Narrative ---
Model Specification This appendix contains the complete mathematical
specification of the ELM -- all ODEs, algebraic intermediates, parameter values,
compound injections, and sex-specific mechanisms. The six coupled ODEs (E1-E6)
define the core dynamics. Seven algebraic intermediates (SIRT1, FOXO3, EZH2,
TSC2, mTORC1, Autophagy, UPRmt) compute instantaneous functions of state. BioAge
is a weighted sum of four state variables, with normalization constants derived
from control simulation at t=1.0. All ~80 active parameters are listed in the
tables at left, organized by subsystem. ITP compound injections and novel
compound injections specify which pathways each drug activates and at what
strength. Sex modifiers implement four distinct biological mechanisms rather
than ad-hoc multipliers.

## SLIDE A17: The Combination Prediction

The Combination Prediction All 8 Taguchi compounds at calibrated doses. The
prediction is remarkable -- but every modeled variable stays within
physiologically plausible bounds. +168% Male +140% Female 8 Compounds Validated:
Rapa + Acarbose share the AMPK pathway -> the model predicted their combination
would be less than the sum of its parts (+33%). The ITP observed +34%. The
saturation logic works. Sanity-checked: We ran the full 8-compound stack and
tracked every internal variable. Nothing blows up. NAD + , heteroplasmy,
senescent cells, methylation -- all stay within ranges the model was calibrated
on. The large extension comes from addressing six independent aging axes
simultaneously, not from any single variable being pushed into fantasy
territory.

--- Narrative ---
Why +168% Isn't As Crazy As It Sounds A number this large demands scepticism.
Two things make it defensible: 1. Nothing is being pushed beyond what we
calibrated. Each compound enters the model at exactly the dose the ITP tested
(or, for novel compounds, at literature-estimated levels). When all eight run
simultaneously, we checked every internal variable: NAD + levels, DNA damage,
heteroplasmy, senescent cell counts. None leaves the range the model was fitted
on by more than 35%. The extension is large because six different bottlenecks
are being relieved at once -- not because any single lever is being cranked to
an implausible setting. 2. Pairwise interactions are boring. We ran all 28 two-
compound pairs. Most behave additively (mean ratio of actual to expected: 1.02).
Five pairs sharing the same pathway show diminishing returns. Eleven cross-
pathway pairs show mild synergy. No pair produces a wildly unexpected result.
The full-stack prediction is the sum of many small, well-behaved pieces. What
this doesn't prove: the model may still be wrong about biology we haven't
measured. Whether these pathways actually combine as predicted in a living
animal is what the Taguchi killifish screen (A24) is designed to test.

## SLIDE A18: Telomere Rivers

Telomere Rivers: New Evidence A single intervention just achieved +65% median
lifespan extension in mice. The biological ceiling is far higher than anyone
thought. What are Rivers? CD4+ T cells donate telomeric vesicles (extracellular
vesicles carrying stemness factors) to aged tissues. These vesicles carry
NOTCH1, beta-catenin, WNT5A, and RUNX2 -- reversing senescence markers across
five organs (liver, kidney, spleen, lung, brain). Detail Value Strain C57BL/6
Treatment age 22 months (~human 65-70) Control median ~29 months Treated median
~47 months Extension +65% Maximum lifespan ~60 months Sample size n = 10 per
group Lanna, Valvo & Dustin, bioRxiv Nov 2025. Important caveats: Preprint, not
yet peer-reviewed. n = 10 per group. Not yet independently replicated. However,
the endpoint (alive vs. dead over 15+ months) is unambiguous -- this is not a
biomarker study.

--- Narrative ---
Why This Matters The authors are from Oxford and UCL -- heavyweight
affiliations. Their prior published work on telomere transfer appeared in Nature
Cell Biology (2022). The mechanism: CPT1A-dependent fatty acid oxidation (FAO)
at the immunological synapse powers telomere transfer. T cells package telomeric
DNA with stemness transcription factors into extracellular vesicles ("Rivers").
These vesicles reach aged tissues and reverse senescence markers. If replicated,
this is the largest lifespan extension from a single intervention in normal
(non-progeroid) mice in the literature.

## SLIDE A19: Rivers as Ceiling Calibrator

Rivers as Ceiling Calibrator How far beyond a normal lifespan does each result
reach? Gray = normal median lifespan. Colour = additional life from
intervention.

--- Narrative ---
The Axes Argument Aging has multiple independent failure modes. Rivers targets
the immune/telomere/stemness axis (1 axis). The ELM stack targets
metabolic/damage/epigenetic/mitochondrial/senescence axes (5-6 axes). If axes
are independent, more coverage should yield more effect. The predicted +168%
from 6 axes is 2.6x the Rivers result from 1 axis -- consistent with diminishing
returns across partially coupled pathways. Mice that naturally die at ~30 months
can survive to at least 47-60 months (Rivers). The Taguchi-8 prediction of +168%
implies ~80 months is an accessible extension of the survival range established
by the Telomere Rivers intervention.

## SLIDE A20: What the Model Misses

What the Model Misses The ELM is metabolic-centric. Entire categories of aging
biology are outside its scope. Regenerative (not modeled) Wnt/beta-catenin
signaling Tissue renewal and stem cell maintenance Notch1 pathway Cell fate
decisions, tissue homeostasis Telomere dynamics Replicative capacity of dividing
cells Senescence reversal Rivers reverses senescence markers (vs. clearance)
EZH2 non-catalytic rejuvenation Separate from methylation writing Immune /
systemic (not modeled) Thymic involution Immune system aging -- T cell
production declines Stem cell exhaustion Tissue regeneration capacity loss
Immune senescence Accumulated dysfunctional immune cells Structural wear (not
