modeled) AGE crosslinks Irreversible collagen crosslinks -- monotonically
accumulating Organ-specific fibrosis Tissue stiffening (heart, lung, kidney)
Protein aggregation Amyloid, lipofuscin -- cumulative, poorly cleared The
unknown unknowns: Some of these failure modes only become rate-limiting once
you've already rescued the earlier ones. We don't know what kills a 10-year-old
mouse -- because there has never been one.

--- Narrative ---
Beyond the ELM's Scope Three independent rejuvenation pathways now exist in the
literature: OSK reprogramming: partial cellular reprogramming (Yamanaka factors)
EZH2 non-catalytic: rejuvenation via scaffolding function, separate from
methylation Rivers: telomeric vesicle transfer from T cells The ELM models only
OSK, and only partially. If any of these stack on top of metabolic rescue, the
true ceiling may exceed +168%, not fall short. Conversely, AGE crosslinks
(advanced glycation end-products) are irreversible collagen modifications that
accumulate monotonically. They may impose a hard ceiling invisible at normal
lifespans but rate-limiting at extended ones.

## SLIDE A21: NAD-Obligate Tissues

The Canaries in the Coal Mine The ELM is NAD-centric. Its predictive power
depends on how much a tissue relies on OXPHOS -- versus having metabolic escape
routes. Tissues with low metabolic flexibility are NAD-obligate: when NAD+
drops, they have nowhere to turn. The ELM models their failure well. Tissues
with high metabolic flexibility can switch fuel sources (fatty acid beta-
oxidation, glycolysis, ketolysis), buffering NAD+ decline for decades. Tissue
Primary fuel Backups Flexibility Aging onset ELM fit Retinal photoreceptors
OXPHOS (extreme) Minimal Very low 40s-50s Strong Cochlear hair cells OXPHOS None
Very low 50s-60s Strong Dopaminergic neurons OXPHOS + ROS load Minimal Low 60s+
Strong Cortical neurons OXPHOS Ketones Low-mod 60s-70s Good Pancreatic beta-
cells OXPHOS-coupled Limited Low-mod 50s-60s Good Kidney prox. tubule OXPHOS
Some Moderate 60s-70s Moderate Cardiomyocytes FAO (~70%) Glc, ketones, lactate
Mod-high 70s+ Needs ext. Hepatocytes Mixed FA, glc, AA, ketones High Preserved
Needs ext. Skeletal muscle FAO + glycolysis Ketones, glc, AA High Late Needs
ext. Gut epithelium Butyrate + glutamine Glucose, SCFAs High Well-preserved
Needs ext. The eyes go dim, the ears lose high frequencies, cognition fades --
while the gut digests and the legs walk. That's not a random clinical
observation. It's exactly what a NAD-centric model predicts.

--- Narrative ---
Domain of Validity The ELM is a model of the canaries in the coal mine -- NAD-
obligate tissues that serve as early warning systems for systemic metabolic
decline. Best predicted: macular degeneration, hearing loss, neurodegenerative
diseases (Parkinson's, Alzheimer's) -- all conditions where OXPHOS-dependent
tissues fail earliest. Needs extension: cardiac aging, liver disease, gut
dysfunction -- tissues where metabolic flexibility buys decades of buffer time.
For metabolically flexible tissues, the model would need: Alternative fuel
pathway capacity terms Substrate switching kinetics Age-dependent degradation of
compensatory mechanisms Sarcopenia does happen -- just decades after presbyopia.
The compensatory mechanisms eventually fail too. The ELM captures the first wave
of failures; extending it to the second wave is a concrete next step.

## SLIDE A22: The Metabolic Gate Hypothesis

The Metabolic Gate Hypothesis The ELM's metabolic variables may gate the body's
endogenous rejuvenation pipeline. If so, metabolic interventions are indirectly
regenerative. Metabolic decline NAD+v, mTOR^, OXPHOSv (the ELM's world) v APC
FAO drops below threshold Antigen-presenting cell fatty acid oxidation hits a
metabolic cliff v Endogenous Rivers collapse Stemness factors lost, telomere
maintenance lost v Accelerated aging More senescence -> more SASP -> feeds back
to top What this means: Rapamycin, NMN, Urolithin A may keep the rejuvenation
pipeline running -- not just rescue metabolic variables. Testable prediction: Do
rapamycin-treated aged mice show higher serum Rivers (telomeric DNA+, NOTCH1+
extracellular vesicles)?

--- Narrative ---
Cliff Dynamics If Rivers production requires fatty acid oxidation (FAO) above a
metabolic threshold -- and the CPT1A dependence suggests it does (Hill
coefficient $n \approx 3\text{-}4$) -- then metabolic decline crossing that
threshold produces sudden regenerative collapse, not gradual fade. This matches
the observed late-life acceleration of aging: systems don't decay linearly, they
fail catastrophically once support systems cross critical thresholds. The gate
hypothesis is modelable as one algebraic intermediate reusing existing ELM state
variables: $\text{Rivers competence} = \text{Hill}(\text{FAO}(\text{NAD},
\text{AMPK}),\; K_m,\; n)$ where $n \approx 3\text{-}4$ captures the cliff.

## SLIDE A23: Known vs. Unknown

What We Know vs. What We Don't The honest answer: the 8-compound stack at
calibrated doses predicts +168% M / +140% F. Here's exactly what we've checked
and what remains uncertain. [done] Checked Rapa+Acarb prediction: +33% vs +34%
observed Biological ceiling >= +65% (Rivers, unreplicated preprint) 10 effective
degrees of freedom from 12 data points Under the full stack, every modeled
variable stays within physiologically plausible bounds All 28 pairwise
combinations behave predictably (mean additivity ratio 1.02) [?] Not Yet Tested
Whether independent pathways compound as predicted in living animals Where the
organism-level ceiling actually is Novel compound dose-response calibration
(NMN, CD38i, D+Q, UroA) What failure modes emerge at 60, 80+ months The
prediction is +168% M / +140% F. The model's internal variables all stay in
range, but 4 of 8 compounds lack lifespan calibration data. The Taguchi
killifish screen (A24) is designed to test this prediction directly.

--- Narrative ---
What Would Narrow It We've confirmed that the model's internals behave sensibly
under the full 8-compound stack -- no variable is pushed into implausible
territory. Two investigations remain: Failure mode census: Survey mouse
pathology literature -- what % of mortality does the ELM's four BioAge
components actually cover? Multi-axis combination data: The Taguchi L18
killifish screen (A24) will directly test whether pathways interact additively,
synergistically, or antagonistically in living animals. Each converts a specific
unknown into a known. The +168% prediction is the testable hypothesis.

## SLIDE A24: Taguchi Combination Testing

Taguchi L18: Combination Longevity Screen in Killifish 8 compounds x mixed dose
levels in a single orthogonal array. One experiment, 8 months: main effects,
dose-response, and a screen for dominant interactions. The unisex rationale Two
ITP compounds (17alpha-estradiol, aspirin) show zero lifespan effect in female
mice . With long-term human translation in mind, we exclude male-only
interventions. The screen uses the 4 ITP compounds effective in both sexes plus
4 novel targets from the ELM's predictions: ITP unisex cocktail (4) Rapa --
mTORC1 -> autophagy Acarb -- AMPK + gut microbiome Cana -- AMPK + antioxidant
Gly -- antioxidant Novel targets (4) NMN -- NAD+ synthesis CD38i -- NAD+
preservation D+Q -- senescent cell clearance UroA -- mitophagy L18 Orthogonal
Array (2^1 x 3^7) -- = vehicle, Lo = low dose, Hi = high dose, [done] = on
(CD38i: 2 levels only) Run Rapa Acarb Cana Gly NMN CD38i D+Q UroA 1 -- -- -- --
-- -- -- -- 2 -- Lo Lo Lo Lo -- Lo Lo 3 -- Hi Hi Hi Hi -- Hi Hi 4 Lo -- -- Lo Lo
-- Hi Hi 5 Lo Lo Lo Hi Hi -- -- -- 6 Lo Hi Hi -- -- -- Lo Lo 7 Hi -- Lo -- Hi --
Lo Hi 8 Hi Lo Hi Lo -- -- Hi -- 9 Hi Hi -- Hi Lo -- -- Lo 10 -- -- Hi Hi Lo
[done] Lo -- 11 -- Lo -- -- Hi [done] Hi Lo 12 -- Hi Lo Lo -- [done] -- Hi 13 Lo
-- Lo Hi -- [done] Hi Lo 14 Lo Lo Hi -- Lo [done] -- Hi 15 Lo Hi -- Lo Hi [done]
Lo -- 16 Hi -- Hi Lo Hi [done] -- Lo 17 Hi Lo -- Hi -- [done] Lo Hi 18 Hi Hi Lo
-- Lo [done] Hi -- 19 Vehicle control 20 Hi Hi Hi Hi Hi [done] Hi Hi 20 groups x
50 fish = 1,000 animals. Runs 1-18: L18 orthogonal array. Run 19: vehicle
control. Run 20: all-8-high (non-orthogonal -- tests the full stack). Turquoise
killifish ( N. furzeri ): vertebrate, 4-6 month lifespan, established aging
model.

--- Narrative ---
Why Taguchi? Genichi Taguchi developed orthogonal arrays for Toyota in the
1950s. The key insight: you don't need to test every possible combination. An
L18 balanced fractional design (2^1 x 3^7 = 18 runs for 8 factors) ensures every
factor level appears equally often, so main effects are cleanly separable from
interactions. Standard practice in manufacturing and pharma (QPOP for oncology
dose optimization). Never systematically applied to aging research -- a genuine
methodological gap. Why these 8 compounds The ITP has validated 6 compounds in
mice, but 17alpha-estradiol requires testosterone (zero female effect) and
aspirin conflicts with estrogen signaling (zero female effect via COX-2->PGI2).
We exclude both: the goal is interventions that work in both sexes, because we
don't want to leave half the human population behind. The 4 remaining ITP
compounds (Rapa, Acarb, Cana, Gly) form a unisex base . The 4 novel targets
(NMN, CD38i, D+Q, UroA) are the ELM's predicted highest-value additions. None of
these have known sex-specific failure modes. Decision-grade deliverables (a)
Main effect of each compound by sex, with 95% confidence intervals (b) Screen
for dominant pairwise interactions, ranked by effect size, with CIs (c)
All-8-high arm verdict: does the full stack break? (toxicity, antagonism, or
runaway synergy) (d) Whether the ITP unisex cocktail translates to killifish
(cross-species portability) Candidate labs: Benayoun (USC), Brunet (Stanford),
Valenzano (Jena) -- all have established killifish colonies. Cost comparison: A
single ITP mouse study costs ~$1.5M over 3 years -- and that assumes mice die on
schedule. If a cocktail achieves even a 2x extension, the confirmatory mouse
study takes 5+ years; at 3-4x, over a decade. The killifish screen (4-6 month
lifespan) delivers combination data in ~8 months for ~$200K, regardless of
effect size. Why combinations are mandatory: See the Serial Reliability slide
(early deck) -- if aging has multiple independent failure modes, single-
intervention testing is structurally incapable of producing large effects or
revealing later failure modes. Operational risk controls Fish randomized to
tanks by stratified body-mass allocation to control husbandry variance. Compound
administration via diet with batch-matched controls across all 20 groups.
Mortality scored blind by two independent observers; discrepancies resolved by a
third. Censoring protocol preregistered: fish removed for non-aging causes
(injury, escaped tank) are right-censored at removal date. Full analysis plan
preregistered before first fish is dosed: primary endpoint (median lifespan),
secondary endpoints (main effects, pairwise interactions), and exploratory
(all-8-high vs. model prediction). The statistical design is robust; these
controls ensure execution artifacts do not undermine it.

## SLIDE A25: Roadmap: Experiment, Then Extend

Roadmap: Experiment, Then Extend The model makes a testable prediction. Now we
need the experiment. Then we let the data tell us what to add. [lab] Phase 1 --
Generate the data that doesn't exist Taguchi L18 killifish screen -- 8
compounds, 20 groups, ~1,000 fish, ~$200K, ~8 months. Directly tests the +168%
prediction and reveals which interactions matter. Rivers + ITP drug interaction
-- do rapamycin-treated aged mice show higher serum telomeric vesicles? Tests
the metabolic gate hypothesis (Slide A22). Confirmatory mouse cohorts for the
top killifish combinations. [wrench] Phase 2 -- Let the data extend the model
Recalibrate against killifish combination outcomes -- which interactions did the
model get right, which did it miss? Add pathways only where new data constrains
them -- Rivers competence gate, immune function, AGE crosslinks. Each requires
an endpoint to fit, not just a literature estimate. Cross-species translation --
killifish -> mouse -> human dose and timescale mapping. The model is called
Extensible for a reason. But extension without data is speculation. The
killifish screen generates the data; the model earns its next set of parameters.
Models without data are stories. Do not add epicycles before data constrains
them.

--- Narrative ---
Why This Order Phase 1 comes first because the model's computational work is
done. Every modeled variable stays in range under the full 8-compound stack. All
28 pairwise combinations behave predictably. The prediction (+168% M / +140% F)
uses only calibrated doses. What's missing is not more modeling -- it's
combination data from living animals. The Taguchi screen is the highest-value
experiment: 8 compounds, orthogonal design, main effects plus dominant
interactions, all in ~8 months. The Rivers experiment (do ITP drugs boost
telomeric vesicles?) is the single highest-information side experiment because
it tests whether the metabolic and regenerative axes are coupled. Phase 2 waits
for results. Adding ODE terms for AGE crosslinks, immune function, or telomere
dynamics before empirical constraints exist would make the model more complex
without making it more predictive. Each new pathway needs an endpoint to
calibrate against -- otherwise it's just a parameter with a literature guess and
no anchor.

## SUPPLEMENTARY: Methylation Fraction of Biological Age
(Referenced by Slide A6: BioAge Weight Sensitivity)

==============================================================================
  METHYLATION FRACTION OF BIOLOGICAL AGE
  Across published multi-component aging clocks
==============================================================================

RATIONALE
----------------------------------------
The ELM's BioAge weights four aging hallmarks: methylation (0.30),
DNA damage (0.30), heteroplasmy (0.25), senescent cells (0.15).
How does this compare to published biological age composites?

METHOD
----------------------------------------
For each clock, map components to ELM-equivalent categories:
  epigenetic   --  age-dependent chromatin/methylation remodeling
  damage       --  DNA/mitochondrial/oxidative damage
  senescence   --  SASP, cellular senescence, immune senescence
  inflammation  --  systemic inflammaging (overlaps senescence)
  metabolic    --  glucose, lipid, adipokine dysregulation
  organ_decline  --  renal, hepatic, pulmonary function loss

Variance contribution = (|beta| x population SD)^2 for weighted clocks,
or equal counts for DunedinPACE (uniform weights).

==============================================================================
  GrimAge2
  Lu et al. 2022, Aging 14(24):9484-9549, PMC9792204
  PMID 36516495
==============================================================================

  Component                   Var contrib  Fraction  Category
  ----------------------------------------------------------------------
  DNAm ADM                         0.2374     10.9%  epigenetic
  DNAm B2M                         0.1751      8.0%  inflammation
  DNAm Cystatin C                  0.6659     30.6%  organ_decline
  DNAm GDF-15                      0.0784      3.6%  damage
  DNAm Leptin                      0.0927      4.3%  metabolic
  DNAm CRP                         0.5792     26.6%  inflammation
  DNAm A1C                         0.0407      1.9%  metabolic
  DNAm PAI-1                       0.1946      8.9%  senescence
  DNAm TIMP-1                      0.0337      1.5%  senescence
  DNAm PACKYRS                     0.0784      3.6%  damage

  Category totals:
    inflammation             34.7%
    organ_decline            30.6%
    epigenetic               10.9%
    senescence               10.5%
    damage                    7.2%
    metabolic                 6.1%

  Strict epigenetic fraction:  10.9%
  Broad (+ 1/2 damage coupling): 14.5%

==============================================================================
  PhenoAge
  Levine et al. 2018, Aging 10(4):573-591, PMC5940111
  PMID 29676998
==============================================================================

  Component                   Var contrib  Fraction  Category
  ----------------------------------------------------------------------
  Albumin                          0.0181      4.1%  organ_decline
  Creatinine                       0.0361      8.2%  organ_decline
  Glucose                          0.0858     19.4%  metabolic
  ln(CRP)                          0.0131      3.0%  inflammation
  Lymphocyte%                      0.0092      2.1%  senescence
  MCV                              0.0180      4.1%  damage
  RDW                              0.2459     55.7%  damage
  Alk Phosphatase                  0.0032      0.7%  organ_decline
  WBC                              0.0123      2.8%  inflammation

  Category totals:
    damage                   59.7%
    metabolic                19.4%
    organ_decline            13.0%
    inflammation              5.7%
    senescence                2.1%

  Strict epigenetic fraction:  0.0%
  Broad (+ 1/2 damage coupling): 29.9%

==============================================================================
  DunedinPACE
  Belsky et al. 2022, eLife 11:e73420, PMC8853656
  PMID 35029144
==============================================================================

  Component                   Var contrib  Fraction  Category
  ----------------------------------------------------------------------
  BMI                              1.0000      5.3%  metabolic
  Waist-hip ratio                  1.0000      5.3%  metabolic
  HbA1c                            1.0000      5.3%  metabolic
  Leptin                           1.0000      5.3%  metabolic
  Total cholesterol                1.0000      5.3%  metabolic
  Triglycerides                    1.0000      5.3%  metabolic
  HDL cholesterol                  1.0000      5.3%  metabolic
  Lipoprotein(a)                   1.0000      5.3%  metabolic
  ApoB/ApoA1                       1.0000      5.3%  metabolic
  MAP                              1.0000      5.3%  organ_decline
  VO2max                           1.0000      5.3%  organ_decline
  FEV1/FVC                         1.0000      5.3%  organ_decline
  FEV1                             1.0000      5.3%  organ_decline
  eGFR                             1.0000      5.3%  organ_decline
  BUN                              1.0000      5.3%  organ_decline
  hs-CRP                           1.0000      5.3%  inflammation
  WBC                              1.0000      5.3%  inflammation
  Perio attachment                 1.0000      5.3%  damage
  Dental caries                    1.0000      5.3%  damage

  Category totals:
    metabolic                47.4%
    organ_decline            31.6%
    inflammation             10.5%
    damage                   10.5%

  Strict epigenetic fraction:  0.0%
  Broad (+ 1/2 damage coupling): 5.3%

==============================================================================
  DRIFT vs COMPOSITE CLOCK MORTALITY PREDICTION
  Hillary et al. 2020, PMID 32736664 (LBC1936 cohort)
==============================================================================

  Clock             HR per SD   Type
  ---------------------------------------------
  Horvath (drift)       1.02       drift
  Hannum (drift)        1.04       drift
  PhenoAge              1.05       composite
  GrimAge               1.10       composite

  Mean log(HR) drift clocks:     0.0295
  Mean log(HR) composite clocks: 0.0721
  Ratio (drift / composite):     0.41
  -> Pure drift captures ~41% of composite mortality signal

==============================================================================
  SUMMARY: METHYLATION FRACTION RANGE
==============================================================================

  GrimAge2         strict=10.9%   broad=14.5%
  PhenoAge         strict=0.0%   broad=29.9%
  DunedinPACE      strict=0.0%   broad=5.3%
  HR-ratio method:        41%

  RANGE: 0% to 30%
  (lower = pure drift share of mortality prediction;
   upper = epigenetic + damage-coupled remodeling in GrimAge)

  ELM current w_meth = 0.30 -- within this range.
  ELM calibration constraint: w_meth in [0.25, 0.40]

CITATIONS
----------------------------------------
  Horvath 2013, Genome Biol 14:R115, PMID 24138928
  Hannum et al. 2013, Mol Cell 49:359-367, PMID 23177740

  Lu et al. 2019, Aging 11(2):303-327, PMID 30669119
  Lu et al. 2022, Aging 14(24):9484-9549, PMID 36516495
  Belsky et al. 2022, eLife 11:e73420, PMID 35029144
  Hillary et al. 2020, Clin Epigenetics 12:57, PMID 32736664
